parts.content: The phase angle is known such that the $\cos\theta$ coordinate is equal to $\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{13}}$. Find all possible values for the $\sin\theta$ coordinate.
parts.markscheme:
- Use the Pythagorean identity: $\sin^2\theta+\cos^2\theta=1 \implies \sin^2\theta=1-\left(\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{13}}\right)^2=\dfrac{9}{13}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Solve for $\sin\theta$: $\sin\theta=\pm\dfrac{3}{\sqrt{13}}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content:
The question gives us the value of the cosine coordinate and asks for the sine coordinate. We are given:

$$\cos\theta = \frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}$$ 

We need to find all possible values for $\sin\theta$.

steps.title: Select the Pythagorean identity
steps.content:
To relate sine and cosine directly, we use the Pythagorean identity found in the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet:

$$\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$$

steps.title: Substitute the known value
steps.content:
Substitute $\cos\theta = \frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}$ into the identity:

$$\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}\right)^2 + \sin^2\theta = 1$$

steps.title: Square the cosine term
steps.content:
Square the fraction $\frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}$:

$$\frac{2^2}{(\sqrt{13})^2} + \sin^2\theta = 1$$ 

$$\frac{4}{13} + \sin^2\theta = 1$$

calculator.description: Calculate the square of the cosine term

steps.title: Solve for sin squared
steps.content:
Rearrange the equation to isolate $\sin^2\theta$ by subtracting $\frac{4}{13}$ from both sides:

$$\begin{aligned} \sin^2\theta &= 1 - \frac{4}{13} \\ \sin^2\theta &= \frac{13}{13} - \frac{4}{13} \\ \sin^2\theta &= \frac{9}{13} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the values of sine
steps.content:
Take the square root of both sides. Since $\sin\theta$ can be positive or negative (depending on the quadrant), we must include $\pm$:

$$\sin\theta = \pm\sqrt{\frac{9}{13}}$$ 

$$\sin\theta = \pm\frac{3}{\sqrt{13}}$$ 

The two possible values are $\frac{3}{\sqrt{13}}$ and $-\frac{3}{\sqrt{13}}$.

steps.title: Interpret cosine as ratio
steps.content:
We are given that $\cos\theta = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{13}}$. 

Using the right-angled triangle definition of cosine, we can set up a triangle where:
$$\cos\theta = \frac{\text{Adjacent}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}$$

So, we construct a right-angled triangle with an **adjacent side** of $2$ and a **hypotenuse** of $\sqrt{13}$.

steps.title: Calculate the opposite side
steps.content:
To find $\sin\theta$, we first need the length of the **opposite side**. We use the Pythagorean theorem ($a^2 + b^2 = c^2$) where $a$ is the adjacent side, $b$ is the opposite side, and $c$ is the hypotenuse.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Opposite}^2 + \text{Adjacent}^2 &= \text{Hypotenuse}^2 \\ \text{Opposite}^2 + 2^2 &= (\sqrt{13})^2 \\ \text{Opposite}^2 + 4 &= 13 \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the square of the opposite side

steps.title: Solve for opposite length
steps.content:
Rearranging to solve for the opposite side:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Opposite}^2 &= 13 - 4 \\ \text{Opposite}^2 &= 9 \\ \text{Opposite} &= \sqrt{9} = 3 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine sine ratio
steps.content:
Now we use the definition of sine for our right-angled triangle:

$$\sin\theta = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{3}{\sqrt{13}}$$

This gives us the magnitude of $\sin\theta$.

steps.title: Find all possible values
steps.content:
Since $\cos\theta$ is positive, the angle $\theta$ could be in the first quadrant (where sine is positive) or the fourth quadrant (where sine is negative). 

The question asks for **all possible values**, so we must include both the positive and negative cases:

$$\sin\theta = \pm\frac{3}{\sqrt{13}}$$

This relates to the Pythagorean identity $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$ often found in **Topic 3: Geometry and Trigonometry**.

steps.title: Express angle using inverse cosine
steps.content:
We start by expressing the phase angle $\theta$ using the inverse cosine function, as requested by the method.

$$ \theta = \arccos\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}\right) $$

This expression represents the angle whose cosine is $\frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}$. To find $\sin(\theta)$, we need to evaluate $\sin\left(\arccos\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}\right)\right)$ exactly.

steps.title: Construct a reference triangle
steps.content:
To find the exact value without relying on decimal approximations, we interpret the inverse cosine geometrically. We construct a right-angled triangle where $\theta$ is one of the angles.

Since $\cos\theta = \frac{\text{Adjacent}}{\text{Hypotenuse}}$, we can set:
- **Adjacent side** $= 2$
- **Hypotenuse** $= \sqrt{13}$

steps.title: Calculate the opposite side
steps.content:
We use the Pythagorean theorem ($a^2 + b^2 = c^2$) to find the length of the opposite side, let's call it $x$.

$$\begin{aligned} x^2 + 2^2 &= (\sqrt{13})^2 \\ x^2 + 4 &= 13 \\ x^2 &= 9 \\ x &= 3 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Evaluate the sine ratio
steps.content:
Now that we know the opposite side is 3, we can determine the magnitude of the sine of the angle.

$$ \sin\theta = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{3}{\sqrt{13}} $$

steps.title: Determine all possible values
steps.content:
The question asks for **all possible values**. Since $\cos\theta$ is positive, the angle $\theta$ could be in Quadrant I or Quadrant IV.

- In **Quadrant I**, $\sin\theta$ is positive.
- In **Quadrant IV**, $\sin\theta$ is negative.

Therefore, considering both possibilities:

$$ \sin\theta = \pm\frac{3}{\sqrt{13}} $$

parts.content: Given that the phase angle lies in the first quadrant, compute the exact values of the double phase angle: $\sin(2\theta)$, $\cos(2\theta)$, and $\tan(2\theta)$.
parts.markscheme:
- State the values of $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ in the first quadrant: $\sin\theta=\dfrac{3}{\sqrt{13}},\,\cos\theta=\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{13}}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate $\sin(2\theta)$: $\sin(2\theta)=2\left(\dfrac{3}{\sqrt{13}}\right)\left(\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{13}}\right)=\dfrac{12}{13}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate $\cos(2\theta)$: $\cos(2\theta)=\cos^2\theta-\sin^2\theta=\dfrac{4}{13}-\dfrac{9}{13}=-\dfrac{5}{13}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate $\tan(2\theta)$: $\tan(2\theta)=\dfrac{12/13}{-5/13}=-\dfrac{12}{5}$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Identify sine and cosine values
steps.content:
First, we identify the values of $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ for the phase angle. Based on the system's state vector components in the first quadrant, we have:

$$\sin\theta = \frac{3}{\sqrt{13}}, \quad \cos\theta = \frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}$$ 

We will use these values to compute the double angle functions.

highlights.text: phase angle lies in the first quadrant

steps.title: Calculate sin(2θ)
steps.content:
To find $\sin(2\theta)$, we use the double angle identity from the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet:

$$ \sin 2\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta $$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} \sin(2\theta) &= 2\left(\frac{3}{\sqrt{13}}\right)\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}\right) \\ &= \frac{12}{13} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate cos(2θ)
steps.content:
Next, we find $\cos(2\theta)$ using the double angle identity:

$$ \cos 2\theta = \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta $$

Substituting the values:

$$\begin{aligned} \cos(2\theta) &= \left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{3}{\sqrt{13}}\right)^2 \\ &= \frac{4}{13} - \frac{9}{13} \\ &= -\frac{5}{13} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate tan(2θ)
steps.content:
Finally, we determine $\tan(2\theta)$ by dividing our previous results:

$$ \tan(2\theta) = \frac{\sin(2\theta)}{\cos(2\theta)} $$

Substitute the values calculated above:

$$\begin{aligned} \tan(2\theta) &= \frac{12/13}{-5/13} \\ &= -\frac{12}{5} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate tangent of theta
steps.content:
From the state vector components derived in the previous part, we have the values for $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ in the first quadrant:

$$\sin\theta = \frac{3}{\sqrt{13}}, \quad \cos\theta = \frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}$$

First, we calculate $\tan\theta$ using the definition $\tan\theta = \frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}$:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{3/\sqrt{13}}{2/\sqrt{13}} = \frac{3}{2}$$

steps.title: Calculate tan(2θ) using identity
steps.content:
We use the double angle identity for tangent, $\tan(2\theta) = \frac{2\tan\theta}{1-\tan^2\theta}$, to find the value of $\tan(2\theta)$.

Substituting $\tan\theta = \frac{3}{2}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \tan(2\theta) &= \frac{2(\frac{3}{2})}{1 - (\frac{3}{2})^2} \\ &= \frac{3}{1 - \frac{9}{4}} \\ &= \frac{3}{\frac{4}{4} - \frac{9}{4}} \\ &= \frac{3}{-\frac{5}{4}} \\ &= 3 \times \left(-\frac{4}{5}\right) \\ &= -\frac{12}{5} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine quadrant of 2θ
steps.content:
Since $\tan\theta = 1.5$ (which is greater than 1), the angle $\theta$ must be greater than $45^\circ$ ($45^\circ < \theta < 90^\circ$).

Therefore, the double angle $2\theta$ will be between $90^\circ$ and $180^\circ$:

$$90^\circ < 2\theta < 180^\circ$$

This places $2\theta$ in the **second quadrant**, where sine is positive and cosine is negative.

steps.title: Construct triangle for sin/cos
steps.content:
We treat $\tan(2\theta) = -\frac{12}{5}$ as $\frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}}$ (ignoring the sign for the triangle dimensions). We have a right-angled triangle with opposite side $12$ and adjacent side $5$.

Calculating the hypotenuse $h$:

$$h = \sqrt{5^2 + 12^2} = \sqrt{25 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13$$

Now we determine the values using the triangle dimensions and the quadrant signs (Q2):

$$\sin(2\theta) = +\frac{\text{opp}}{\text{hyp}} = \frac{12}{13}$$

$$\cos(2\theta) = -\frac{\text{adj}}{\text{hyp}} = -\frac{5}{13}$$

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The exact values for the double phase angle are:

$$\sin(2\theta) = \frac{12}{13}, \quad \cos(2\theta) = -\frac{5}{13}, \quad \tan(2\theta) = -\frac{12}{5}$$

steps.title: Identify state vector components
steps.content:
From the problem context (as indicated in the markscheme), we identify the specific values for the state vector components $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ in the first quadrant:

$$\cos\theta = \frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}, \quad \sin\theta = \frac{3}{\sqrt{13}}$$ 

We verify these are valid components on the unit circle since $\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{3}{\sqrt{13}}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{13} + \frac{9}{13} = 1$.

steps.title: Define complex number representation
steps.content:
Using the **Complex Number Squaring** method, we represent the state vector as a complex number $z$:

$$z = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta$$

To find the double angle components, we will square this complex number. By De Moivre's theorem (or simply the property of arguments adding), squaring $z$ doubles the angle:

$$z^2 = \cos(2\theta) + i\sin(2\theta)$$

steps.title: Expand the squared complex number
steps.content:
Now we expand $z^2 = (\cos\theta + i\sin\theta)^2$ algebraically:

$$\begin{aligned} z^2 &= (\cos\theta + i\sin\theta)(\cos\theta + i\sin\theta) \\ &= \cos^2\theta + i\sin\theta\cos\theta + i\sin\theta\cos\theta + i^2\sin^2\theta \\ &= \cos^2\theta + 2i\sin\theta\cos\theta - \sin^2\theta \\ &= (\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta) + i(2\sin\theta\cos\theta) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Equate real and imaginary parts
steps.content:
We compare the real and imaginary parts of our expansion with $z^2 = \cos(2\theta) + i\sin(2\theta)$ to isolate the double angle formulas:

**Real Part:**
$$\cos(2\theta) = \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta$$

**Imaginary Part:**
$$\sin(2\theta) = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$$

steps.title: Calculate sine of double angle
steps.content:
Substitute the values $\sin\theta = \frac{3}{\sqrt{13}}$ and $\cos\theta = \frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}$ into the imaginary part equation:

$$\begin{aligned} \sin(2\theta) &= 2\left(\frac{3}{\sqrt{13}}\right)\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}\right) \\ &= 2\left(\frac{6}{13}\right) \\ &= \frac{12}{13} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate cosine of double angle
steps.content:
Substitute the values into the real part equation:

$$\begin{aligned} \cos(2\theta) &= \left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{3}{\sqrt{13}}\right)^2 \\ &= \frac{4}{13} - \frac{9}{13} \\ &= -\frac{5}{13} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate tangent of double angle
steps.content:
Finally, compute $\tan(2\theta)$ using the ratio of sine to cosine:

$$\begin{aligned} \tan(2\theta) &= \frac{\sin(2\theta)}{\cos(2\theta)} \\ &= \frac{12/13}{-5/13} \\ &= -\frac{12}{5} \end{aligned}$$

parts.content: Confirm that the identity $1+\cot^2(2\theta)=\csc^2(2\theta)$ holds true for the calculated double angle values.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute into the LHS: $1+\cot^2(2\theta) = 1+\left(-\dfrac{5}{12}\right)^2=1+\dfrac{25}{144}=\dfrac{169}{144}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute into the RHS: $\csc^2(2\theta)=\dfrac{1}{(12/13)^2}=\dfrac{169}{144}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify values from previous work
steps.content:
To verify the identity using the **LHS and RHS Evaluation** approach, we first retrieve the specific double angle values calculated in the previous parts of the question (as indicated by the markscheme).

We have:
- $\cot(2\theta) = -\frac{5}{12}$
- $\sin(2\theta) = \frac{12}{13}$

Recall that $\csc(2\theta)$ is the reciprocal of $\sin(2\theta)$.

steps.title: Evaluate the Left-Hand Side (LHS)
steps.content:
Substitute the value of $\cot(2\theta)$ into the left side of the identity $1+\cot^2(2\theta)$.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{LHS} &= 1 + \left(-\frac{5}{12}\right)^2 \\ &= 1 + \frac{25}{144} \\ &= \frac{144}{144} + \frac{25}{144} \\ &= \frac{169}{144} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the value of the LHS to verify the fraction:

steps.title: Evaluate the Right-Hand Side (RHS)
steps.content:
Now substitute the value of $\sin(2\theta)$ into the right side. Since $\csc(x) = \frac{1}{\sin(x)}$, we use $\csc^2(2\theta) = \frac{1}{\sin^2(2\theta)}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{RHS} &= \csc^2(2\theta) \\ &= \frac{1}{(\frac{12}{13})^2} \\ &= \frac{1}{\frac{144}{169}} \\ &= \frac{169}{144} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the value of the RHS to verify it matches:

steps.title: Confirm the identity
steps.content:
Compare the results from the LHS and RHS calculations.

$$\text{LHS} = \frac{169}{144} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{RHS} = \frac{169}{144}$$ 

Since $\text{LHS} = \text{RHS}$, the identity $1+\cot^2(2\theta)=\csc^2(2\theta)$ is confirmed for these values.

steps.title: Retrieve calculated values
steps.content:
This question asks us to confirm the identity for the "calculated double angle values" found in previous parts of the problem. Based on the markscheme, these values are:

$$\sin(2\theta) = \frac{12}{13} \quad \text{and} \quad \cos(2\theta) = -\frac{5}{13}$$

steps.title: Rewrite LHS using definitions
steps.content:
First, we look at the Left Hand Side (LHS) of the identity. We rewrite $\cot(2\theta)$ using the definition $\cot A = \frac{\cos A}{\sin A}$.

$$\text{LHS} = 1 + \cot^2(2\theta) = 1 + \left( \frac{\cos(2\theta)}{\sin(2\theta)} \right)^2$$

steps.title: Substitute and simplify LHS
steps.content:
Substitute the values of $\cos(2\theta)$ and $\sin(2\theta)$ into the expression and simplify.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{LHS} &= 1 + \left( \frac{-5/13}{12/13} \right)^2 \\ &= 1 + \left( -\frac{5}{12} \right)^2 \\ &= 1 + \frac{25}{144} \\ &= \frac{144}{144} + \frac{25}{144} \\ &= \frac{169}{144} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the calculation and select the fraction display option.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression and use Frac to convert the decimal to a fraction.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression and click the fraction icon next to the result.

calculator.description: Calculate the LHS value and convert to a fraction

steps.title: Rewrite RHS using definitions
steps.content:
Now we look at the Right Hand Side (RHS). We rewrite $\csc(2\theta)$ using the reciprocal definition $\csc A = \frac{1}{\sin A}$.

$$\text{RHS} = \csc^2(2\theta) = \left( \frac{1}{\sin(2\theta)} \right)^2$$

steps.title: Substitute and simplify RHS
steps.content:
Substitute the value $\sin(2\theta) = \frac{12}{13}$ into the expression.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{RHS} &= \left( \frac{1}{12/13} \right)^2 \\ &= \left( \frac{13}{12} \right)^2 \\ &= \frac{169}{144} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate the inverse of sine squared.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to check equality.

calculator.description: Calculate the RHS value to verify it matches

steps.title: Confirm the identity
steps.content:
Comparing the results from Step 2 and Step 4, we see that both sides are equal.

$$\text{LHS} = \frac{169}{144} = \text{RHS}$$

parts.content:
The measured vibration amplitude $A$ is defined as $A = (\sin\theta+\cos\theta)^2$.
Over a full cycle ($0\le\theta<2\pi$), determine the **maximum** and **minimum** possible values of $A$, giving exact values.
parts.markscheme:
- Expand and simplify the expression for $A$: $A = \sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta + 2\sin\theta\cos\theta = 1+\sin(2\theta)$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Determine the maximum value (when $\sin(2\theta)=1$): $A_{\text{max}}=1+1=2$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Determine the minimum value (when $\sin(2\theta)=-1$): $A_{\text{min}}=1+(-1)=0$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Expand the squared expression
steps.content:
To express $A$ in a simpler form, we first expand the squared binomial $(\sin\theta + \cos\theta)^2$ using the algebraic identity $(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$:

$$A = \sin^2\theta + 2\sin\theta\cos\theta + \cos^2\theta$$

steps.title: Use Pythagorean identity
steps.content:
We can group the squared terms together. From the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet, we apply the Pythagorean identity:

$$\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$$

Substituting this into our expression:

$$A = 1 + 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$$

steps.title: Apply double angle identity
steps.content:
Next, we simplify the term $2\sin\theta\cos\theta$ using the double angle identity for sine, also found in the **Trigonometry** section:

$$\sin 2\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$$

This gives us the simplified function for $A$:

$$A = 1 + \sin(2\theta)$$

steps.title: Analyze the range of sine
steps.content:
The sine function, $\sin(x)$, always oscillates between $-1$ and $1$. This remains true for $\sin(2\theta)$ as long as the domain covers at least one full period.

The given domain $0 \le \theta < 2\pi$ covers two full periods of $\sin(2\theta)$, so it will reach its maximum and minimum values.

$$-1 \le \sin(2\theta) \le 1$$

steps.title: Calculate maximum and minimum
steps.content:
To find the maximum and minimum values of $A$, we substitute the maximum and minimum values of $\sin(2\theta)$ into our simplified equation $A = 1 + \sin(2\theta)$.

**Maximum:**
When $\sin(2\theta) = 1$:
$$A_{\text{max}} = 1 + 1 = 2$$

**Minimum:**
When $\sin(2\theta) = -1$:
$$A_{\text{min}} = 1 + (-1) = 0$$

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content: The maximum value is **2** and the minimum value is **0**.

steps.title: Analyze the expression
steps.content:
We need to find the maximum and minimum values of the amplitude $A$ given by the equation:

$$A = (\sin\theta + \cos\theta)^2$$

To do this using the **Compound Angle Transformation**, we will first express the sum inside the brackets, $\sin\theta + \cos\theta$, as a single sine function in the form $R\sin(\theta + \alpha)$.

steps.title: Calculate R
steps.content:
For an expression of the form $a\sin\theta + b\cos\theta$, the amplitude $R$ is calculated using the formula $R = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$.

Here, we have $1\sin\theta + 1\cos\theta$, so $a=1$ and $b=1$:

$$R = \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{2}$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate square root of sum of squares

steps.title: Determine the phase shift
steps.content:
We write the expression in the form $R\sin(\theta + \alpha)$. Expanding this using the **Compound angle identity** for sine gives:

$$R(\sin\theta\cos\alpha + \cos\theta\sin\alpha) = (R\cos\alpha)\sin\theta + (R\sin\alpha)\cos\theta$$

Comparing this to $1\sin\theta + 1\cos\theta$, we find:
- $R\cos\alpha = 1$
- $R\sin\alpha = 1$

Dividing these gives $\tan\alpha = 1$. Since sine and cosine are positive, $\alpha$ is in the first quadrant:

$$\alpha = \frac{\pi}{4}$$

So, the transformed expression is:

$$\sin\theta + \cos\theta = \sqrt{2}\sin\left(\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$

steps.title: Substitute and square
steps.content:
Now we substitute this single trigonometric term back into our original equation for $A$ and square it:

$$\begin{aligned} A &= \left(\sqrt{2}\sin\left(\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right)^2 \\ A &= (\sqrt{2})^2 \cdot \sin^2\left(\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) \\ A &= 2\sin^2\left(\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the maximum value
steps.content:
To find the maximum value of $A$, we look at the range of the sine squared function. The maximum value of $\sin^2(x)$ is $1$.

Therefore, the maximum value of $A$ is:

$$A_{\text{max}} = 2(1) = 2$$

This occurs when $\sin(\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}) = \pm 1$.

ti84.instructions: Multiply 2 by the maximum value of sine squared

steps.title: Find the minimum value
steps.content:
To find the minimum value of $A$, we consider that a real number squared cannot be negative. The minimum value of $\sin^2(x)$ is $0$.

Therefore, the minimum value of $A$ is:

$$A_{\text{min}} = 2(0) = 0$$

This occurs when $\sin(\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}) = 0$.

ti84.instructions: Multiply 2 by the minimum value of sine squared

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The maximum and minimum possible values of $A$ are:

Maximum: **2**
Minimum: **0**

steps.title: Simplify the expression for A
steps.content:
To make differentiation easier, we first expand and simplify the expression for $A$ using trigonometric identities from the data booklet.

First, expand the square:
$$A = (\sin\theta + \cos\theta)^2 = \sin^2\theta + 2\sin\theta\cos\theta + \cos^2\theta$$

Group the squared terms and use the identity $\cos^{2}\theta+\sin^{2}\theta=1$ and the double angle identity $\sin 2\theta=2\sin\theta\cos\theta$:
$$A = (\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta) + (2\sin\theta\cos\theta)$$
$$A = 1 + \sin(2\theta)$$

steps.title: Differentiate with respect to θ
steps.content:
Using the **Calculus Approach**, we find the derivative of $A$ with respect to $\theta$ to locate the stationary points. We use the chain rule on the term $\sin(2\theta)$.

$$\frac{dA}{d\theta} = \frac{d}{d\theta}(1 + \sin(2\theta))$$

Since the derivative of a constant is 0 and the derivative of $\sin(u)$ is $\cos(u) \cdot u'$:
$$\frac{dA}{d\theta} = 0 + \cos(2\theta) \cdot 2$$
$$\frac{dA}{d\theta} = 2\cos(2\theta)$$

steps.title: Find critical points
steps.content:
Set the derivative to zero to find the critical points where the maximum and minimum values occur:

$$2\cos(2\theta) = 0$$
$$\cos(2\theta) = 0$$

For the domain $0 \le \theta < 2\pi$, the argument $2\theta$ ranges from $0$ to $4\pi$. The cosine function is zero at odd multiples of $\frac{\pi}{2}$:
$$2\theta = \frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3\pi}{2}, \frac{5\pi}{2}, \frac{7\pi}{2}$$

Solving for $\theta$:
$$\theta = \frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{3\pi}{4}, \frac{5\pi}{4}, \frac{7\pi}{4}$$

steps.title: Evaluate A at critical points
steps.content:
Substitute these critical values back into the expression $A = 1 + \sin(2\theta)$ to find the maximum and minimum values.

For $\theta = \frac{\pi}{4}$ and $\theta = \frac{5\pi}{4}$ (where $2\theta = \frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{5\pi}{2}$):
$$A = 1 + \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) = 1 + 1 = 2$$

For $\theta = \frac{3\pi}{4}$ and $\theta = \frac{7\pi}{4}$ (where $2\theta = \frac{3\pi}{2}, \frac{7\pi}{2}$):
$$A = 1 + \sin\left(\frac{3\pi}{2}\right) = 1 + (-1) = 0$$

Therefore, the **maximum** value is 2 and the **minimum** value is 0.

parts.content: Find the values of $\theta$ (in radians, $0\le\theta<2\pi$) for which the amplitude $A$ is greater than $0$.
parts.markscheme:
- Set the condition $A > 0$: $1+\sin(2\theta)>0 \implies \sin(2\theta)>-1$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Identify values where $\sin(2\theta)=-1$: $2\theta=\dfrac{3\pi}{2},\,\dfrac{7\pi}{2}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Solve for $\theta$: $\theta=\dfrac{3\pi}{4},\,\dfrac{7\pi}{4}$

- State the final set of values: $[0, 2\pi) \setminus \left\{\dfrac{3\pi}{4}, \dfrac{7\pi}{4}\right\}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Establish the condition
steps.content:
To find where the amplitude $A$ is greater than $0$, we use the condition derived from the expression for amplitude. Based on the markscheme, the amplitude depends on $1+\sin(2\theta)$.

We require:
$$1+\sin(2\theta) > 0$$

Rearranging gives:
$$\sin(2\theta) > -1$$

steps.title: Analyze the inequality
steps.content:
We know that the sine function has a minimum value of $-1$. Therefore, $\sin(2\theta) \ge -1$ is always true.

The strictly inequality $1+\sin(2\theta) > 0$ holds for all values of $\theta$ **except** where $\sin(2\theta) = -1$.

So, we solve the boundary equation $\sin(2\theta) = -1$ to find the values to exclude.

steps.title: Adjust the domain
steps.content:
The domain for $\theta$ is given as $0 \le \theta < 2\pi$. Since our equation involves the double angle $2\theta$, we must adjust the domain:

$$0 \le 2\theta < 4\pi$$

We need to find all solutions for $2\theta$ within this extended range.

steps.title: Solve for the double angle
steps.content:
In the unit circle, sine is equal to $-1$ at $\frac{3\pi}{2}$. We find all values in the domain $[0, 4\pi)$ by adding the period $2\pi$ to the principal value.

First cycle ($0$ to $2\pi$):
$$2\theta = \frac{3\pi}{2}$$

Second cycle ($2\pi$ to $4\pi$):
$$2\theta = \frac{3\pi}{2} + 2\pi = \frac{7\pi}{2}$$

3. Type sin(7*pi/2) and press EXE

calculator.description: Verify the sine values for the calculated angles

steps.title: Solve for theta
steps.content:
Divide the values of $2\theta$ by 2 to find $\theta$:

For $2\theta = \frac{3\pi}{2}$:
$$\theta = \frac{3\pi}{4}$$

For $2\theta = \frac{7\pi}{2}$:
$$\theta = \frac{7\pi}{4}$$

steps.title: State the final set
steps.content:
The amplitude is greater than $0$ for all valid $\theta$ except for the two values found above. We express this as the set difference.

$$[0, 2\pi) \setminus \left\{\frac{3\pi}{4}, \frac{7\pi}{4}\right\}$$

steps.title: Establish the condition
steps.content:
The question asks for the values of $\theta$ where the amplitude $A$ is greater than $0$. Based on the problem context, this requires the condition:

$$1 + \sin(2\theta) > 0$$

steps.title: Apply identity transformation
steps.content:
We can rewrite the left side using two standard identities from the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet.

First, replace $1$ using the Pythagorean identity:
$$\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$$

Next, expand the double angle sine:
$$\sin 2\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$$

Substituting these into our inequality:
$$(\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta) + 2\sin\theta\cos\theta > 0$$

This forms a perfect square:
$$(\sin\theta + \cos\theta)^2 > 0$$

steps.title: Simplify the condition
steps.content:
Since the square of any real number is always non-negative (i.e., $(\dots)^2 \ge 0$), the strict inequality $(\sin\theta + \cos\theta)^2 > 0$ holds true for all values **except** where the term inside is zero.

So, we must exclude values where:
$$\sin\theta + \cos\theta = 0$$

Or simply require:
$$\sin\theta + \cos\theta \neq 0$$

steps.title: Solve for theta
steps.content:
To solve $\sin\theta + \cos\theta = 0$, we rearrange the terms:
$$\sin\theta = -\cos\theta$$

Dividing both sides by $\cos\theta$ gives:
$$\tan\theta = -1$$

We need to find the values in the domain $0 \le \theta < 2\pi$ to exclude. The reference angle is $\frac{\pi}{4}$, and tangent is negative in the 2nd and 4th quadrants:

*   **Quadrant 2:** $\theta = \pi - \frac{\pi}{4} = \frac{3\pi}{4}$
*   **Quadrant 4:** $\theta = 2\pi - \frac{\pi}{4} = \frac{7\pi}{4}$

steps.content:
The amplitude is positive for all values of $\theta$ in the given domain except for the two values we found.

$$[0, 2\pi) \setminus \left\{\frac{3\pi}{4}, \frac{7\pi}{4}\right\}$$

steps.title: Establish the condition
steps.content:
To find where the amplitude $A$ is greater than $0$, we use the condition derived from the system's properties (likely $A^2 = 1 + \sin(2\theta)$). We need:

$$1 + \sin(2\theta) > 0$$

Rearranging this inequality gives:

$$\sin(2\theta) > -1$$

steps.title: Analyze the function graphically
steps.content:
Using the **Graphical Approach**, we sketch the function $y = \sin(2\theta)$.

We know that the sine function oscillates between $-1$ and $1$. The condition $\sin(2\theta) > -1$ is true almost everywhere, **except** where the graph touches its minimum value of $-1$. 

We need to find these specific points in the domain $0 \le \theta < 2\pi$ to exclude them.

steps.title: Identify critical values
steps.content:
From the graph or unit circle knowledge, the sine function $\sin(u)$ reaches its minimum of $-1$ at $u = \frac{3\pi}{2}$ plus multiples of $2\pi$.

Since our argument is $2\theta$, we set:

$$\begin{aligned} 2\theta &= \frac{3\pi}{2} \\ 2\theta &= \frac{3\pi}{2} + 2\pi = \frac{7\pi}{2} \end{aligned}$$

Note: The next value would be $\frac{11\pi}{2}$, which corresponds to $\theta = \frac{11\pi}{4} > 2\pi$, so it is outside our domain.

steps.title: Solve for theta
steps.content:
Now, divide these values by 2 to find $\theta$:

$$\begin{aligned} \theta &= \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{3\pi}{2}\right) = \frac{3\pi}{4} \\ \theta &= \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{7\pi}{2}\right) = \frac{7\pi}{4} \end{aligned}$$

These are the only two points in the domain $[0, 2\pi)$ where the amplitude is exactly zero.

steps.title: State the final set
steps.content:
Since we want $A > 0$ (strictly greater), we include all values in the domain except for these two points.

The solution set is:

$$[0, 2\pi) \setminus \left\{\frac{3\pi}{4}, \frac{7\pi}{4}\right\}$$

parts.content: $\cos x$ and $\tan x$
parts.markscheme:
- Use Pythagorean identity: $\cos x = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2 x}$
- Substitute $p$: $\cos x = \sqrt{1 - p^2}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Use tangent identity: $\tan x = \frac{\sin x}{\cos x}$
- Substitute expressions: $\tan x = \frac{p}{\sqrt{1 - p^2}}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Apply Pythagorean identity
steps.content:
To find an expression for $\cos x$, we use the fundamental Pythagorean identity from the data booklet:

$$\cos^2 \theta + \sin^2 \theta = 1$$

Substituting the given value $\sin x = p$ into this equation:

$$\cos^2 x + p^2 = 1$$

steps.title: Solve for cos x
steps.content:
Rearrange the equation to solve for $\cos x$:

$$\begin{aligned} \cos^2 x &= 1 - p^2 \\ \cos x &= \pm\sqrt{1 - p^2} \end{aligned}$$

The question specifies that $x$ is an **acute angle** ($0 < x < 90^\circ$), which means $\cos x$ must be positive. Therefore:

$$\cos x = \sqrt{1 - p^2}$$

steps.title: Apply tangent identity
steps.content:
To find $\tan x$, we use the ratio identity that defines tangent in terms of sine and cosine:

$$\tan x = \frac{\sin x}{\cos x}$$

Substitute $\sin x = p$ and our result for $\cos x$:

$$\tan x = \frac{p}{\sqrt{1 - p^2}}$$ 

These are the two required expressions.

steps.title: Interpret sine as a ratio
steps.content:
To solve this using a right-angled triangle, first recall the definition of sine from SOH CAH TOA:

$$ \sin x = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} $$

The question gives us $\sin x = p$. We can write this as a fraction:

$$ \sin x = \frac{p}{1} $$

steps.title: Construct the triangle
steps.content:
Imagine a right-angled triangle with an acute angle $x$. Based on the ratio from the previous step, we can assign the side lengths:

*   **Opposite side** = $p$
*   **Hypotenuse** = $1$

Now we need to find the length of the adjacent side to determine the other ratios.

steps.title: Find the adjacent side
steps.content:
Use the Pythagorean theorem ($a^2 + b^2 = c^2$) to find the unknown adjacent side. Let the adjacent side be $A$.

$$\begin{aligned} A^2 + (\text{Opposite})^2 &= (\text{Hypotenuse})^2 \\ A^2 + p^2 &= 1^2 \\ A^2 &= 1 - p^2 \\ A &= \sqrt{1 - p^2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find cos x
steps.content:
Now we use the definition of cosine:

$$ \cos x = \frac{\text{Adjacent}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} $$

Substitute the values we found from our triangle:

$$ \cos x = \frac{\sqrt{1 - p^2}}{1} = \sqrt{1 - p^2} $$

steps.title: Find tan x
steps.content:
Finally, use the definition of tangent:

$$ \tan x = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} $$

Substitute the side lengths from our triangle:

$$ \tan x = \frac{p}{\sqrt{1 - p^2}} $$

parts.content: $\cos 2x$
parts.markscheme:
- Use double angle identity for cosine: $\cos 2x = 1 - 2\sin^2 x$
- Substitute $p$: $\cos 2x = 1 - 2p^2$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

methods.label: Using Sine Version of Double Angle Identity

steps.title: Select the double angle identity
steps.content:
Since we are given $\sin x = p$ and need to find $\cos 2x$, the most direct approach is to use the double angle identity for cosine expressed in terms of sine. 

While the data booklet provides the standard form $\cos 2\theta = \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta$, we can use the equivalent "sine version" which is often more useful for substitution:

$$ \cos 2x = 1 - 2\sin^2 x $$

steps.title: Substitute p into the identity
steps.content:
We are given that $\sin x = p$. We can substitute $p$ directly into our identity in place of $\sin x$:

$$ \cos 2x = 1 - 2(p)^2 $$

steps.title: State the final expression
steps.content:
Simplifying the expression gives our final answer in terms of $p$:

$$ \cos 2x = 1 - 2p^2 $$

steps.title: Verify with a specific value
steps.content:
We can check our result using a known angle. Let's choose $x = 30^{\circ}$.

1.  **Calculate $p$:** $p = \sin(30^{\circ}) = 0.5$
2.  **Calculate LHS ($\\cos 2x$):** $\cos(2 \times 30^{\circ}) = \cos(60^{\circ}) = 0.5$
3.  **Calculate RHS ($1 - 2p^2$):** $1 - 2(0.5)^2 = 1 - 2(0.25) = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$

Since $0.5 = 0.5$, our expression is correct.

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content: We are given that $\sin x = p$ and need to find an expression for $\cos 2x$ in terms of $p$. To do this, we will use the double angle identity for cosine.

steps.title: Select double angle identity
steps.content: First, we select the fundamental double angle identity for cosine from the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet.

steps.title: Apply Pythagorean identity
steps.content: Since our goal is to write the expression in terms of $p$ (which is $\sin x$), we need to replace the $\cos^2 x$ term. We use the Pythagorean identity from the data booklet.

steps.title: Substitute and simplify
steps.content:
Rearranging the Pythagorean identity gives $\cos^2 x = 1 - \sin^2 x$. We substitute this into the double angle formula and then replace $\sin x$ with $p$:

$$\begin{aligned} \cos 2x &= \cos^2 x - \sin^2 x \\ &= (1 - \sin^2 x) - \sin^2 x \\ &= 1 - 2\sin^2 x \\ &= 1 - 2p^2 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The expression for $\cos 2x$ in terms of $p$ is:

$$1 - 2p^2$$

parts.content: $\sin 2x$
parts.markscheme:
- Use double angle identity for sine: $\sin 2x = 2\sin x \cos x$
- Substitute known expressions: $\sin 2x = 2p\sqrt{1 - p^2}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the double angle formula
steps.content:
To find an expression for $\sin 2x$, we use the double angle identity for sine found in the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet.

$$\sin 2\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$$

Substituting $x$ for $\theta$, we get:
$$\sin 2x = 2\sin x \cos x$$

steps.title: Find expression for cos x
steps.content:
We are given that $\sin x = p$. To use the double angle formula, we need to find an expression for $\cos x$ in terms of $p$.

We use the Pythagorean identity from the data booklet:
$$\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$$

Rearranging for $\cos x$:
$$\begin{aligned} \cos^2 x + (\sin x)^2 &= 1 \\ \cos^2 x + p^2 &= 1 \\ \cos^2 x &= 1 - p^2 \\ \cos x &= \pm\sqrt{1 - p^2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine the sign of cos x
steps.content:
Since the question states that $x$ is an **acute angle** (meaning $0 < x < 90^\circ$ or $0 < x < \frac{\pi}{2}$), it falls in the first quadrant where cosine is positive.

Therefore, we take the positive root:
$$\cos x = \sqrt{1 - p^2}$$

steps.title: Substitute into double angle formula
steps.content:
Now we substitute $\sin x = p$ and $\cos x = \sqrt{1 - p^2}$ back into the double angle formula from Step 0.

$$\begin{aligned} \sin 2x &= 2(\sin x)(\cos x) \\ \sin 2x &= 2(p)(\sqrt{1 - p^2}) \\ \sin 2x &= 2p\sqrt{1 - p^2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Construct reference triangle
steps.content:
Since $\sin x = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = p$, we can represent this ratio by sketching a right-angled triangle with angle $x$.

Let the opposite side be $p$ and the hypotenuse be $1$.

steps.title: Find adjacent side
steps.content:
Use the Pythagorean theorem ($a^2 + b^2 = c^2$) to find the length of the adjacent side.

$$\begin{aligned} (\text{adjacent})^2 + (\text{opposite})^2 &= (\text{hypotenuse})^2 \\ (\text{adjacent})^2 + p^2 &= 1^2 \\ (\text{adjacent})^2 &= 1 - p^2 \\ \text{adjacent} &= \sqrt{1 - p^2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine cos x
steps.content:
Using the triangle, we can now find an expression for $\cos x$.

$$\cos x = \frac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = \frac{\sqrt{1 - p^2}}{1} = \sqrt{1 - p^2}$$

steps.title: Identify double angle formula
steps.content: To find $\sin 2x$, we use the double angle identity from the data booklet.

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Substitute the expressions for $\sin x$ and $\cos x$ into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} \sin 2x &= 2(\sin x)(\cos x) \\ \sin 2x &= 2(p)(\sqrt{1 - p^2}) \\ \sin 2x &= 2p\sqrt{1 - p^2} \end{aligned}$$

parts.content: $\tan 2x$
parts.markscheme:
- Use identity: $\tan 2x = \frac{\sin 2x}{\cos 2x}$
- Substitute expressions from previous parts: $\tan 2x = \frac{2p\sqrt{1 - p^2}}{1 - 2p^2}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: State the ratio identity
steps.content:
To find $\tan 2x$, we can express it as the ratio of sine to cosine:

$$ \tan 2x = \frac{\sin 2x}{\cos 2x} $$

steps.title: Identify double angle formulas
steps.content:
Next, we replace the numerator and denominator using the double angle formulas found in the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet:

$$ \sin 2x = 2\sin x\cos x $$
$$ \cos 2x = \cos^2 x - \sin^2 x $$

steps.title: Substitute into the fraction
steps.content:
Substitute these formulas back into our expression for $\tan 2x$:

$$ \tan 2x = \frac{2\sin x \cos x}{\cos^2 x - \sin^2 x} $$

steps.title: Find cosine in terms of p
steps.content:
We are given that $\sin x = p$. To evaluate the expression, we also need to express $\cos x$ in terms of $p$. Using the Pythagorean identity:

$$ \cos^2 x + \sin^2 x = 1 $$
$$ \cos^2 x = 1 - p^2 $$
$$ \cos x = \sqrt{1 - p^2} $$

steps.title: Substitute values to solve
steps.content:
Finally, substitute $\sin x = p$, $\cos x = \sqrt{1-p^2}$, and $\cos^2 x = 1-p^2$ into our fraction:

$$\begin{aligned} \tan 2x &= \frac{2(p)(\sqrt{1-p^2})}{(1-p^2) - p^2} \\ &= \frac{2p\sqrt{1-p^2}}{1 - 2p^2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find cos x in terms of p
steps.content:
First, we need to find an expression for $\cos x$. Using the Pythagorean identity $\cos^2 x + \sin^2 x = 1$:

$$\cos^2 x = 1 - \sin^2 x$$

Substituting $\sin x = p$:

$$\cos^2 x = 1 - p^2$$

Since $x$ is an acute angle, $\cos x$ is positive:

$$\cos x = \sqrt{1 - p^2}$$

steps.title: Determine tan x
steps.content:
Now we find $\tan x$ using the definition $\tan x = \frac{\sin x}{\cos x}$:

$$\tan x = \frac{p}{\sqrt{1 - p^2}}$$ 

We will use this expression in the double angle formula.

steps.title: Apply tangent double angle formula
steps.content:
We use the double angle identity for tangent:

$$\tan 2x = \frac{2\tan x}{1 - \tan^2 x}$$

Substituting our expression for $\tan x$:

$$\tan 2x = \frac{2\left(\frac{p}{\sqrt{1 - p^2}}\right)}{1 - \left(\frac{p}{\sqrt{1 - p^2}}\right)^2}$$

steps.title: Simplify the denominator
steps.content:
First, simplify the denominator $1 - \tan^2 x$:

$$\begin{aligned} 1 - \left(\frac{p}{\sqrt{1 - p^2}}\right)^2 &= 1 - \frac{p^2}{1 - p^2} \\ &= \frac{1 - p^2}{1 - p^2} - \frac{p^2}{1 - p^2} \\ &= \frac{1 - 2p^2}{1 - p^2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Simplify the final expression
steps.content:
Now substitute this back into the expression for $\tan 2x$:

$$\begin{aligned} \tan 2x &= \frac{\frac{2p}{\sqrt{1 - p^2}}}{\frac{1 - 2p^2}{1 - p^2}} \\ &= \frac{2p}{\sqrt{1 - p^2}} \times \frac{1 - p^2}{1 - 2p^2} \end{aligned}$$

Since $\frac{1 - p^2}{\sqrt{1 - p^2}} = \sqrt{1 - p^2}$, this simplifies to:

$$\tan 2x = \frac{2p\sqrt{1 - p^2}}{1 - 2p^2}$$

parts.content: $\sin 4x$
parts.markscheme:
- Use double angle identity: $\sin 4x = 2\sin 2x \cos 2x$
- Substitute expressions: $\sin 4x = 2\left(2p\sqrt{1 - p^2}\right)\left(1 - 2p^2\right)$
- Simplify: $\sin 4x = 4p\sqrt{1 - p^2}\left(1 - 2p^2\right)$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Use double angle identity
steps.content:
To find an expression for $\sin 4x$, we treat $4x$ as $2(2x)$ and apply the sine double angle identity from the data booklet.

$$\sin 2\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$$

Substituting $\theta = 2x$:

$$\sin 4x = 2\sin 2x \cos 2x$$

steps.title: Express single terms in p
steps.content:
We need to express everything in terms of $p$. We are given that $\sin x = p$. Since $x$ is an acute angle, $\cos x$ is positive.

Using the Pythagorean identity $\cos^2 x + \sin^2 x = 1$:

$$\begin{aligned} \cos x &= \sqrt{1 - \sin^2 x} \\ &= \sqrt{1 - p^2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Expand sin(2x) and cos(2x)
steps.content:
Now we apply double angle identities again to break down the $2x$ terms into $x$ terms.

For $\sin 2x$:
$$\sin 2x = 2\sin x \cos x = 2p\sqrt{1 - p^2}$$

For $\cos 2x$, we use the identity involving only sine, as it's simpler given $\sin x = p$. Since $\cos 2x = \cos^2 x - \sin^2 x$ and $\cos^2 x = 1 - \sin^2 x$:
$$\begin{aligned} \cos 2x &= 1 - 2\sin^2 x \\ &= 1 - 2p^2 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute and simplify
steps.content:
Finally, substitute the expressions for $\sin 2x$ and $\cos 2x$ back into our equation for $\sin 4x$:

$$\begin{aligned} \sin 4x &= 2(\sin 2x)(\cos 2x) \\ &= 2\left(2p\sqrt{1 - p^2}\right)\left(1 - 2p^2\right) \\ &= 4p\sqrt{1 - p^2}(1 - 2p^2) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Verify with a value
steps.content:
You can verify your algebraic result by testing a specific angle. Let $x = 30^\circ$, so $p = \sin(30^\circ) = 0.5$.

Left side: $\sin(4 \times 30^\circ) = \sin(120^\circ) \approx 0.866$

Right side expression:
$$4(0.5)\sqrt{1 - 0.5^2}(1 - 2(0.5)^2)$$
$$2\sqrt{0.75}(1 - 0.5)$$
$$2(0.866)(0.5) \approx 0.866$$

The values match, confirming our expression is correct.

gdc.instructions: Calculate sin(120) first, then evaluate the algebraic expression with p=0.5

ti84.instructions: Calculate sin(120) first, then evaluate the algebraic expression with p=0.5

desmos.instructions: Type the expressions into separate lines to compare values

calculator.description: Calculate sin(120) and the expression with p=0.5 to verify they match.

steps.title: Apply De Moivre's Theorem
steps.content:
To find an expression for $\sin 4x$, we start by using **De Moivre's Theorem** with $n=4$. The theorem states that for any integer $n$, $(\cos x + i\sin x)^n = \cos(nx) + i\sin(nx)$.

Setting $n=4$:
$$(\cos x + i\sin x)^4 = \cos 4x + i\sin 4x$$

steps.title: Expand the complex expression
steps.content:
Next, we expand the left side $(\cos x + i\sin x)^4$ using the **Binomial Theorem**. The coefficients for the 4th power are $1, 4, 6, 4, 1$.

$$\begin{aligned} (\cos x + i\sin x)^4 &= \cos^4 x + 4\cos^3 x (i\sin x) + 6\cos^2 x (i\sin x)^2 \\ &\quad + 4\cos x (i\sin x)^3 + (i\sin x)^4 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Simplify powers of i
steps.content:
Now we simplify the powers of $i$, remembering that $i^2 = -1$, $i^3 = -i$, and $i^4 = 1$.

$$\begin{aligned} (\cos x + i\sin x)^4 &= \cos^4 x + i(4\cos^3 x \sin x) - 6\cos^2 x \sin^2 x \\ &\quad - i(4\cos x \sin^3 x) + \sin^4 x \end{aligned}$$

Grouping the real and imaginary parts:
$$(\cos^4 x - 6\cos^2 x \sin^2 x + \sin^4 x) + i(4\cos^3 x \sin x - 4\cos x \sin^3 x)$$

steps.title: Equate imaginary parts
steps.content:
By equating the imaginary part of our expansion to the imaginary part of $\cos 4x + i\sin 4x$, we get an expression for $\sin 4x$:

$$\sin 4x = 4\cos^3 x \sin x - 4\cos x \sin^3 x$$

To make substitution easier, we can factor out $4\cos x \sin x$:

$$\sin 4x = 4\cos x \sin x (\cos^2 x - \sin^2 x)$$

steps.title: Substitute p
steps.content:
We are given $\sin x = p$. We need to express $\cos x$ in terms of $p$. Using the Pythagorean identity $\cos^2 x + \sin^2 x = 1$:

$$\cos^2 x = 1 - p^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos x = \sqrt{1 - p^2}$$

steps.title: Final substitution and simplification
steps.content:
Substitute $\sin x = p$, $\cos x = \sqrt{1-p^2}$, and $\cos^2 x = 1-p^2$ into our factored expression:

$$\begin{aligned} \sin 4x &= 4(\sqrt{1-p^2})(p) ((1 - p^2) - p^2) \\ &= 4p\sqrt{1-p^2}(1 - 2p^2) \end{aligned}$$

questions.specification: Let $A$ be an obtuse angle in a triangle such that $\sin A = \frac{5}{13}$.

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Given sin A, you must determine cos A (without finding A explicitly) using trig relationships and quadrant/obtuse information before applying double-angle formulas.

parts.content: Find the value of $\sin 2A$ and $\cos 2A$.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate $\cos A$: using $\sin^2 A + \cos^2 A = 1$, we get $\cos A = \pm \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{5}{13}\right)^2} = \pm \frac{12}{13}$
- Since $A$ is obtuse, $\cos A = -\frac{12}{13}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Substitute into double angle formula for sine: $\sin 2A = 2 \sin A \cos A = 2 \left(\frac{5}{13}\right) \left(-\frac{12}{13}\right)$
- $\sin 2A = -\frac{120}{169}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Substitute into double angle formula for cosine: $\cos 2A = 1 - 2 \sin^2 A = 1 - 2\left(\frac{5}{13}\right)^2$ <marks>M1</marks>
- $\cos 2A = \frac{119}{169}$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Use the Pythagorean identity
steps.content:
To find $\sin 2A$ and $\cos 2A$, we first need to determine the value of $\cos A$. We can use the Pythagorean identity from the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet:

$$\cos^{2}\theta + \sin^{2}\theta = 1$$

Rearranging for $\cos^2 A$:

$$\cos^2 A = 1 - \sin^2 A$$

steps.title: Calculate the value of cos A
steps.content:
Substitute $\sin A = \frac{5}{13}$ into the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} \cos^2 A &= 1 - \left(\frac{5}{13}\right)^2 \\ &= 1 - \frac{25}{169} \\ &= \frac{169}{169} - \frac{25}{169} \\ &= \frac{144}{169} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Type 1 - (5/13)^2 and press ENTER. Use Math > Frac to convert to a fraction.

steps.title: Determine the sign of cos A
steps.content:
Taking the square root gives $\cos A = \pm \frac{12}{13}$. 

The question states that $A$ is an **obtuse angle** (between $90^\circ$ and $180^\circ$). In the second quadrant, the cosine function is negative.

Therefore:
$$\cos A = -\frac{12}{13}$$

steps.title: Find sin 2A using double angle formula
steps.content:
Now we use the double angle formula for sine from the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet:

$$\sin 2\theta = 2 \sin \theta \cos \theta$$

Substitute the values $\sin A = \frac{5}{13}$ and $\cos A = -\frac{12}{13}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \sin 2A &= 2\left(\frac{5}{13}\right)\left(-\frac{12}{13}\right) \\ &= \frac{2 \times 5 \times -12}{169} \\ &= -\frac{120}{169} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Type 2 * (5/13) * (-12/13) and press ENTER. Convert to fraction.

steps.title: Find cos 2A using double angle formula
steps.content:
Finally, we use the double angle formula for cosine. While there are three versions, we can use the main one from the data booklet:

$$\cos 2\theta = \cos^{2}\theta - \sin^{2}\theta$$

Substitute $\cos A = -\frac{12}{13}$ and $\sin A = \frac{5}{13}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \cos 2A &= \left(-\frac{12}{13}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{5}{13}\right)^2 \\ &= \frac{144}{169} - \frac{25}{169} \\ &= \frac{119}{169} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Type (-12/13)^2 - (5/13)^2 and press ENTER. Convert to fraction.

steps.title: State the final answers
steps.content:
The values are:

$$\sin 2A = -\frac{120}{169}$$

$$\cos 2A = \frac{119}{169}$$

steps.title: Set up reference triangle
steps.content:
To find the trigonometric ratios, we first visualize a right-angled reference triangle based on the given value $\sin A = \frac{5}{13}$.

Recall that $\sin A = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$.

Therefore, we can set:
- Opposite side $= 5$
- Hypotenuse $= 13$

steps.title: Find the adjacent side
steps.content:
We use the Pythagorean theorem, $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$, to find the length of the adjacent side ($x$).

$$\begin{aligned} x^2 + 5^2 &= 13^2 \\ x^2 + 25 &= 169 \\ x^2 &= 144 \\ x &= 12 \end{aligned}$$

So, the adjacent side length is 12.

gdc.instructions: Compute the square root of (hypotenuse squared minus opposite squared)

ti84.instructions: Compute the square root of (hypotenuse squared minus opposite squared)

desmos.instructions: Compute the square root of (hypotenuse squared minus opposite squared)

calculator.description: Calculate the missing side using Pythagoras

steps.title: Determine sign of cos A
steps.content:
Now we determine the value of $\cos A = \frac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$. The magnitude is $\frac{12}{13}$.

We must check the quadrant to find the correct sign. The question states that $A$ is an **obtuse angle**, which means $90^\circ < A < 180^\circ$. This places angle $A$ in the **second quadrant**.

Using the CAST rule (or unit circle):
- In Quadrant II, only Sine is positive.
- Cosine is negative.

Therefore:
$$\cos A = -\frac{12}{13}$$

steps.title: Calculate sin 2A
steps.content:
We use the double angle formula for sine from the data booklet.

Using $\sin A = \frac{5}{13}$ and $\cos A = -\frac{12}{13}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \sin 2A &= 2 \sin A \cos A \\ &= 2 \left(\frac{5}{13}\right) \left(-\frac{12}{13}\right) \\ &= -\frac{120}{169} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate cos 2A
steps.content:
We use the double angle formula for cosine. You can use any of the three forms given in the booklet or derived from it. Let's use $\cos(2A) = 1 - 2\sin^{2}(A)$ (or $\cos^{2}(A) - \sin^{2}(A)$).

$$\begin{aligned} \cos(2A) &= 1 - 2\sin^{2}(A) \\ &= 1 - 2\left(\frac{5}{13}\right)^{2} \\ &= 1 - 2\left(\frac{25}{169}\right) \\ &= 1 - \frac{50}{169} \\ &= \frac{169}{169} - \frac{50}{169} \\ &= \frac{119}{169} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State final answers
steps.content:
The values are:

$$\sin 2A = -\frac{120}{169}$$

$$\cos 2A = \frac{119}{169}$$

parts.content: The orbital mechanics dictate that the $\sin\theta$ component is equal to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ at a given point in the orbit. Find all possible values of the $\cos\theta$ component.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute $\sin\theta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ into $\sin^2\theta+\cos^2\theta=1$: $\cos^2\theta=1-\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2=\frac{1}{2}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- State the possible values: $\cos\theta=\pm\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Select Pythagorean identity
steps.content: To find $\cos\theta$ when given $\sin\theta$, we use the fundamental Pythagorean identity found in the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet.

steps.title: Substitute sine value
steps.content: Substitute the given value $\sin\theta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ into the identity.

steps.title: Solve for cosine squared
steps.content:
Square the sine value and subtract it from 1 to isolate $\cos^2\theta$.

$$\begin{aligned} \cos^2\theta + \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 &= 1 \\ \cos^2\theta + \frac{1}{2} &= 1 \\ \cos^2\theta &= 1 - \frac{1}{2} \\ \cos^2\theta &= \frac{1}{2} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the value of 1 minus the square of the sine component

steps.title: Determine possible values
steps.content:
Take the square root of both sides to find the possible values for $\cos\theta$. Remember to include both the positive and negative roots, as the satellite could be in different quadrants.

$$\cos\theta = \pm\sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} = \pm\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

This question uses the Pythagorean identity covered in **Topic 3.6: Pythagorean identity**.

steps.title: Identify the reference angle
steps.content: We are given that the sine component is $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$. From our knowledge of the **Unit Circle** and standard angles (often found in **Topic 3: Geometry and Trigonometry**), we recognize that a sine of $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ corresponds to a reference angle of $\frac{\pi}{4}$ (or $45^\circ$).

gdc.instructions: Use the trig menu to select asin. Enter the fraction.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [SIN] to get arcsin. Enter 1 / [2nd] [x^2] (for sqrt) 2.

desmos.instructions: Type arcsin followed by the value.

calculator.description: Calculate the reference angle using the inverse sine function

steps.title: Determine the valid quadrants
steps.content:
On the unit circle, $\sin\theta$ represents the $y$-coordinate. Since the given value $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ is positive, the angle $\theta$ must lie in the quadrants where the $y$-value is positive:

*   **Quadrant I**
*   **Quadrant II**

steps.title: Find cosine in Quadrant I
steps.content:
In Quadrant I, the angle is $\theta = \frac{\pi}{4}$. The cosine value ($x$-coordinate) is positive.

$$\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$ 

(Note: This is approximately $0.707$)

ti84.instructions: Press [COS]. Enter the angle (in degrees or radians matching your mode).

calculator.description: Verify the cosine of the reference angle

steps.title: Find cosine in Quadrant II
steps.content:
In Quadrant II, the corresponding angle is $\theta = \pi - \frac{\pi}{4} = \frac{3\pi}{4}$ (or $135^\circ$). In this quadrant, the cosine value ($x$-coordinate) is negative, but has the same magnitude as the reference angle.

$$\cos\left(\frac{3\pi}{4}\right) = -\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$ 

(Note: This is approximately $-0.707$)

calculator.description: Verify the cosine of the second quadrant angle

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Combining the results from both possible quadrants, the possible values for the $\cos\theta$ component are:

$$\cos\theta = \pm\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$ 

This method confirms the result required by the markscheme.

parts.content: If the satellite is in the range corresponding to the second quadrant, compute the exact values of the double angle terms: $\sin(2\theta)$, $\cos(2\theta)$, and $\tan(2\theta)$.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify values in quadrant II: $\sin\theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ and $\cos\theta=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate $\sin(2\theta) = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta = 2\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = -1$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate $\cos(2\theta) = \cos^2\theta-\sin^2\theta = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2} = 0$ <marks>A1</marks>
- State $\tan(2\theta)$ is undefined <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Identify sine and cosine values
steps.content:
First, we identify the specific coordinate values for the satellite's position in the second quadrant. Based on the problem context (corresponding to a position like $(-1, 1)$ or an angle of $135^\circ$), the normalized coordinates are:

$$\sin\theta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \quad \cos\theta = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$ 

We verify these satisfy the unit circle condition: $\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 + \left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} = 1$.

steps.title: Calculate sin(2θ)
steps.content:
To find $\sin(2\theta)$, we use the double angle identity from the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet. Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} \sin(2\theta) &= 2\sin\theta\cos\theta \\ &= 2\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \\ &= 2\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right) \\ &= -1 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate cos(2θ)
steps.content:
Next, we find $\cos(2\theta)$ using its double angle identity. We substitute the values of $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$:

$$\begin{aligned} \cos(2\theta) &= \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta \\ &= \left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 \\ &= \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2} \\ &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine tan(2θ)
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate $\tan(2\theta)$ using the ratio of sine to cosine:

$$\tan(2\theta) = \frac{\sin(2\theta)}{\cos(2\theta)} = \frac{-1}{0}$$ 

Since division by zero is not possible, $\tan(2\theta)$ is **undefined**.

steps.title: Identify the angle θ
steps.content:
First, we identify the specific angle $\theta$ in the **second quadrant** corresponding to the coordinate values $\sin\theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ and $\cos\theta=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.

The reference angle with these magnitudes is $45^\circ$ (or $\frac{\pi}{4}$). Since the satellite is in the second quadrant, the angle is:

$$\theta = 180^\circ - 45^\circ = 135^\circ$$

Alternatively, in radians:
$$\theta = \pi - \frac{\pi}{4} = \frac{3\pi}{4}$$

steps.title: Calculate the double angle
steps.content:
Next, we simply double the angle $\theta$ to find $2\theta$.

$$2\theta = 2 \times 135^\circ = 270^\circ$$

Or in radians:
$$2\theta = 2 \times \frac{3\pi}{4} = \frac{6\pi}{4} = \frac{3\pi}{2}$$

steps.title: Evaluate sine and cosine
steps.content:
Now we evaluate the sine and cosine functions directly for the angle $270^\circ$ (or $\frac{3\pi}{2}$). On the unit circle, this angle corresponds to the point $(0, -1)$.

For sine:
$$\sin(2\theta) = \sin(270^\circ) = -1$$

For cosine:
$$\cos(2\theta) = \cos(270^\circ) = 0$$

steps.title: Evaluate tangent
steps.content:
Finally, we evaluate the tangent function for $270^\circ$. Since $\tan(x) = \frac{\sin(x)}{\cos(x)}$, we have:

$$\tan(2\theta) = \frac{-1}{0}$$

Division by zero is undefined. Therefore, $\tan(2\theta)$ is **undefined**.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The exact values are:

*   $\sin(2\theta) = -1$
*   $\cos(2\theta) = 0$
*   $\tan(2\theta)$ is undefined

parts.content: Confirm that the identity $\cos(2\theta)=2\cos^2\theta-1$ holds true for the calculated double angle values.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute the value into LHS: $\cos(2\theta)=0$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute the value into RHS: $2\cos^2\theta-1 = 2\left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2-1 = 2\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)-1 = 0$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify known values
steps.content:
From the satellite's position in the previous parts (indicated by the markscheme values), we work with the angle $\theta = \frac{3\pi}{4}$ (or $135^\circ$).

At this position:
$$\cos\theta = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$ 

We need to verify the identity using this value.

steps.title: Evaluate the Left Hand Side (LHS)
steps.content:
First, we evaluate the LHS of the identity $\cos(2\theta)$ by doubling the angle.

Since $\theta = \frac{3\pi}{4}$, the double angle is:
$$2\theta = 2 \times \frac{3\pi}{4} = \frac{3\pi}{2} \quad (\text{or } 270^\circ)$$

Now, find the cosine of this angle:
$$\text{LHS} = \cos\left(\frac{3\pi}{2}\right) = 0$$

gdc.instructions:
1. In Run-Matrix mode, select [OPTN] > [ANGLE] > [Deg]
2. Enter cos(2 * 135) and press [EXE]

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [MODE] and ensure Degree mode is selected
2. Press [COS] [2] [*] [1] [3] [5] [)] [ENTER]

calculator.description: Calculate cos(2 * 135) to verify the LHS value

steps.title: Evaluate the Right Hand Side (RHS)
steps.content:
Next, we substitute the value of $\cos\theta$ into the RHS expression $2\cos^2\theta-1$.

Substitute $\cos\theta = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$:
$$\begin{aligned} \text{RHS} &= 2\left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 - 1 \\ &= 2\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) - 1 \\ &= 1 - 1 \\ &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the RHS value using the value of cos(theta)

steps.title: Confirm the identity
steps.content:
Compare the results from both sides.

Since $\text{LHS} = 0$ and $\text{RHS} = 0$, the identity is confirmed:
$$\cos(2\theta) = 2\cos^2\theta - 1$$

parts.content:
The required thrust $T$ is given by the equation $T = (\sin\theta-\cos\theta)^2$.

Over a full orbit ($0\le\theta<2\pi$), determine the **maximum** and **minimum** possible thrusts, giving exact values.
parts.markscheme:
- Expand the expression: $T = \sin^2\theta - 2\sin\theta\cos\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1 - \sin(2\theta)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate maximum thrust when $\sin(2\theta)=-1$: $T_{\text{max}} = 1 - (-1) = 2$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate minimum thrust when $\sin(2\theta)=1$: $T_{\text{min}} = 1 - (1) = 0$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Expand the thrust equation
steps.content:
To apply the **Trigonometric Expansion** method, we first expand the squared binomial in the thrust equation $T = (\sin\theta - \cos\theta)^2$:

$$T = \sin^2\theta - 2\sin\theta\cos\theta + \cos^2\theta$$

steps.title: Simplify using identities
steps.content:
Next, we simplify the expanded expression using two formulas from the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet:
1. The Pythagorean identity: $$\cos^2\theta+\sin^2\theta=1$$
2. The double angle identity: $$\sin 2\theta=2\sin\theta\cos\theta$$

Substituting these into our equation:

$$\begin{aligned} T &= (\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta) - (2\sin\theta\cos\theta) \\ T &= 1 - \sin(2\theta) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine max and min
steps.content:
The sine function $\sin(2\theta)$ always has a range of $[-1, 1]$. We use this property to find the maximum and minimum thrusts.

**Maximum Thrust:**
To maximize $T = 1 - \sin(2\theta)$, we need to subtract the smallest possible value ($-1$):
$$T_{\text{max}} = 1 - (-1) = 2$$

**Minimum Thrust:**
To minimize $T = 1 - \sin(2\theta)$, we subtract the largest possible value ($1$):
$$T_{\text{min}} = 1 - (1) = 0$$

Thus, the maximum thrust is 2 and the minimum thrust is 0.

steps.title: Simplify the function T
steps.content:
Before applying calculus, it is much easier to differentiate if we simplify the expression for thrust first. Expand the squared term using algebraic expansion $(a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$:

$$T = (\sin\theta - \cos\theta)^2 = \sin^2\theta - 2\sin\theta\cos\theta + \cos^2\theta$$

Now, substitute the trigonometric identities from the data booklet to simplify:
- $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$
- $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin(2\theta)$

$$T = 1 - \sin(2\theta)$$

steps.title: Differentiate with respect to θ
steps.content:
To find the maximum and minimum, we need to find the critical points where the rate of change is zero. Differentiate $T$ with respect to $\theta$:

$$\frac{dT}{d\theta} = \frac{d}{d\theta}(1 - \sin(2\theta))$$

Using the chain rule, where the derivative of $\sin(u)$ is $\cos(u) \cdot u'$:

$$\frac{dT}{d\theta} = -2\cos(2\theta)$$

steps.title: Find critical points
steps.content:
Set the derivative to zero to find the critical values of $\theta$:

$$-2\cos(2\theta) = 0$$
$$\cos(2\theta) = 0$$

For the domain $0 \le \theta < 2\pi$, the argument $2\theta$ ranges from $0$ to $4\pi$. The cosine function is zero at $\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3\pi}{2}, \frac{5\pi}{2}, \frac{7\pi}{2}$.

Solving for $\theta$:
$$2\theta = \frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow \theta = \frac{\pi}{4}$$
$$2\theta = \frac{3\pi}{2} \Rightarrow \theta = \frac{3\pi}{4}$$
$$2\theta = \frac{5\pi}{2} \Rightarrow \theta = \frac{5\pi}{4}$$
$$2\theta = \frac{7\pi}{2} \Rightarrow \theta = \frac{7\pi}{4}$$

gdc.instructions:

2. Enter y1 = (sin(x) - cos(x))^2
3. Set View Window x from 0 to 2π
4. Draw graph
5. Use G-Solve > Max and G-Solve > Min

calculator.description: Visualize the function to confirm extrema locations

steps.title: Evaluate T at critical points
steps.content:
Substitute these critical values back into the original or simplified equation $T = 1 - \sin(2\theta)$ to find the thrust:

For $\theta = \frac{\pi}{4}$ and $\frac{5\pi}{4}$:
$$T = 1 - \sin\left(2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}\right) = 1 - \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) = 1 - 1 = 0$$

For $\theta = \frac{3\pi}{4}$ and $\frac{7\pi}{4}$:
$$T = 1 - \sin\left(2 \cdot \frac{3\pi}{4}\right) = 1 - \sin\left(\frac{3\pi}{2}\right) = 1 - (-1) = 2$$

(Note: Since the function is periodic and continuous on the closed domain loop, these critical points represent the global extrema.)

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The question asks for the exact values.

**Maximum thrust:** $$2$$
**Minimum thrust:** $$0$$

steps.title: Strategy: Harmonic Form
steps.content:
To find the maximum and minimum values of $T = (\sin\theta - \cos\theta)^2$, we will first simplify the inner expression $\sin\theta - \cos\theta$ using the **Harmonic Form Transformation**.

We express it in the form:
$$R\sin(\theta - \alpha)$$

steps.title: Calculate amplitude R
steps.content:
For an expression of the form $a\sin\theta - b\cos\theta$, the amplitude $R$ is calculated using the formula $R = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$.

Identifying the coefficients $a=1$ and $b=1$ from $\sin\theta - \cos\theta$:
$$R = \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{2}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate square root of sum of squares

ti84.instructions: Calculate square root of sum of squares

steps.title: Calculate phase angle alpha
steps.content:
We find the angle $\alpha$ by equating the coefficients to the expansion of $R\sin(\theta - \alpha)$:
$$R\sin(\theta - \alpha) = R(\sin\theta\cos\alpha - \cos\theta\sin\alpha)$$

Comparing this to $1\sin\theta - 1\cos\theta$, we have:
$$R\cos\alpha = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad R\sin\alpha = 1$$

Dividing sine by cosine gives $\tan\alpha$:
$$\tan\alpha = \frac{1}{1} = 1$$

Since both coefficients are positive, $\alpha$ is in the first quadrant:
$$\alpha = \frac{\pi}{4}$$

steps.title: Rewrite the function
steps.content:
Substituting $R = \sqrt{2}$ and $\alpha = \frac{\pi}{4}$ back into the expression:
$$\sin\theta - \cos\theta = \sqrt{2}\sin\left(\theta - \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$

Now, substitute this into the equation for thrust $T$:
$$T = \left[\sqrt{2}\sin\left(\theta - \frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right]^2$$
$$T = 2\sin^2\left(\theta - \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$

steps.title: Determine maximum thrust
steps.content:
To maximize $T = 2\sin^2\left(\theta - \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$, we need the maximum possible value of the $\sin^2$ function.

The range of $\sin(x)$ is $[-1, 1]$, so the maximum value of $\sin^2(x)$ is $1$.

$$T_{\text{max}} = 2(1) = 2$$

This gives us the first required value.

desmos.instructions: Graph function to see max height

steps.title: Determine minimum thrust
steps.content:
To minimize $T = 2\sin^2\left(\theta - \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$, we look for the minimum value of the squared term.

Since a squared real number is always non-negative, the minimum value of $\sin^2(x)$ is $0$.

$$T_{\text{min}} = 2(0) = 0$$

This gives us the second required value.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
The exact maximum and minimum thrusts are:

**Maximum:** $2$
**Minimum:** $0$

parts.content: Find the values of $\theta$ (in radians, $0\le\theta<2\pi$) for which the thrust $T$ is exactly $2$.
parts.markscheme:
- Set thrust equation equal to 2: $1-\sin(2\theta)=2 \Rightarrow \sin(2\theta)=-1$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Find values for $2\theta$: $2\theta=\frac{3\pi}{2}, \frac{7\pi}{2}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Solve for $\theta$: $\theta=\frac{3\pi}{4}, \frac{7\pi}{4}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
The question asks for the values of $\theta$ where the thrust $T$ is exactly 2. Using the expression for thrust (implied from the context of the problem and markscheme), we set up the equation:

$$1 - \sin(2\theta) = 2$$

steps.title: Isolate the sine term
steps.content:
To solve for $\theta$, we first need to isolate the trigonometric term $\sin(2\theta)$.

Subtract 1 from both sides:
$$-\sin(2\theta) = 1$$

Multiply by $-1$:
$$\sin(2\theta) = -1$$

steps.title: Adjust the domain
steps.content:
The question specifies the domain for $\theta$ as $0 \le \theta < 2\pi$. Since our argument is $2\theta$, we must adjust the domain to find all possible solutions.

Multiply the inequality by 2:
$$0 \le 2\theta < 4\pi$$

steps.title: Solve for the argument
steps.content:
We need to find the values of the angle $2\theta$ where the sine is $-1$ within the range $[0, 4\pi)$.

On the unit circle, $\sin(x) = -1$ at $x = \frac{3\pi}{2}$.

Since our domain goes up to $4\pi$ (two full rotations), we find the second solution by adding $2\pi$ to the first solution:

$$2\theta = \frac{3\pi}{2}, \quad \frac{3\pi}{2} + 2\pi = \frac{7\pi}{2}$$

steps.title: Find the values of theta
steps.content:
Finally, divide by 2 to solve for $\theta$:

$$\begin{aligned} \theta &= \frac{\frac{3\pi}{2}}{2} = \frac{3\pi}{4} \\ \theta &= \frac{\frac{7\pi}{2}}{2} = \frac{7\pi}{4} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The values of $\theta$ for which the thrust is 2 are:

$$\theta = \frac{3\pi}{4}, \frac{7\pi}{4}$$

Set this expression equal to 2:
$$1 - \sin(2\theta) = 2$$

Using the calculator's intersection tool, identify the $x$-coordinates where the curve $y_1$ touches the line $y_2$. The graph shows that the curve touches the line $y=2$ at its maximum points.

steps.title: Convert to exact values
steps.content:
The question asks for the values of $\theta$. While the calculator gives decimal approximations, these values correspond to exact fractions of $\pi$. Recognizing these or converting the decimals gives:

$$\begin{aligned} 2.356 \dots &= \frac{3\pi}{4} \\ 5.498 \dots &= \frac{7\pi}{4} \end{aligned}$$

This matches the markscheme which requires identifying these specific angles.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The values of $\theta$ for which the thrust is exactly 2 are:

$$\theta = \frac{3\pi}{4}, \frac{7\pi}{4}$$

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
We are asked to find $\theta$ when the thrust $T$ is exactly $2$. Based on the markscheme, the equation for thrust is $1 - \sin(2\theta)$.

Set the expression equal to $2$:
$$1 - \sin(2\theta) = 2$$

steps.title: Isolate the trigonometric term
steps.content:
Rearrange the equation to isolate $\sin(2\theta)$:

$$\begin{aligned} -\sin(2\theta) &= 2 - 1 \\ -\sin(2\theta) &= 1 \\ \sin(2\theta) &= -1 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Apply double angle identity
steps.content:
Use the double angle identity for sine from the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet to expand $\sin(2\theta)$.

$$2\sin\theta\cos\theta = -1$$

steps.title: Create a perfect square
steps.content:
Rearrange the equation to equal zero:

$$2\sin\theta\cos\theta + 1 = 0$$

Now, use the Pythagorean identity from the data booklet to replace $1$ with $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta$:

$$\begin{aligned} 2\sin\theta\cos\theta + (\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta) &= 0 \\ \sin^2\theta + 2\sin\theta\cos\theta + \cos^2\theta &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Factorize the equation
steps.content:
Recognize that the expression is a perfect square of the form $(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$:
$$(\sin\theta + \cos\theta)^2 = 0$$

Take the square root of both sides:
$$\sin\theta + \cos\theta = 0$$

steps.title: Convert to tangent
steps.content:
Rearrange to solve for $\theta$. Subtract $\cos\theta$ from both sides:
$$\sin\theta = -\cos\theta$$

Divide by $\cos\theta$ (assuming $\cos\theta \neq 0$) to form a tangent equation:
$$\frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} = -1 \implies \tan\theta = -1$$

steps.title: Solve for theta
steps.content:
We need to find $\theta$ where $\tan\theta = -1$ in the domain $0 \le \theta < 2\pi$.

The reference angle is $\frac{\pi}{4}$ (since $\tan(\frac{\pi}{4}) = 1$). Tangent is negative in the **2nd** and **4th** quadrants.

**Quadrant 2:**
$$\theta_1 = \pi - \frac{\pi}{4} = \frac{3\pi}{4}$$

**Quadrant 4:**
$$\theta_2 = 2\pi - \frac{\pi}{4} = \frac{7\pi}{4}$$

Final Answer:
$$\theta = \frac{3\pi}{4}, \frac{7	\pi}{4}$$

questions.specification:
A point $P$ moves around the unit circle centered at the origin $O(0,0)$ with coordinates $P(\cos\theta,\sin\theta)$.

At a certain instant, the $x$-coordinate of $P$ is $-\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$.
Find all possible values of $\sin\theta$.

parts.markscheme:
- From identity $\sin^2\theta+\cos^2\theta=1\Rightarrow \sin^2\theta=1-(-\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3})^2=1-\tfrac{3}{9}=\tfrac{6}{9}=\tfrac{2}{3}$. <marks>M1</marks>
- $\sin\theta=\pm\sqrt{\dfrac{2}{3}}=\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{3}$. <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: If $P$ lies in the second quadrant, find the exact values of $\sin(2\theta)$, $\cos(2\theta)$, and $\tan(2\theta)$.
parts.markscheme:
- In quadrant II: $\sin\theta=\tfrac{\sqrt{6}}{3},\,\cos\theta=-\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$. <marks>M1</marks>
- $\sin(2\theta)=2\sin\theta\cos\theta=2(\tfrac{\sqrt{6}}{3})(-\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3})=-\tfrac{2\sqrt{18}}{9}=-\tfrac{6\sqrt{2}}{9}=-\tfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{3}.$ <marks>A1</marks>
- $\cos(2\theta)=2\cos^2\theta-1=2(\tfrac{3}{9})-1=2(\tfrac{1}{3})-1=-\tfrac{1}{3}.$ <marks>A1</marks>
- $\tan(2\theta)=\dfrac{\sin(2\theta)}{\cos(2\theta)}=\dfrac{-2\sqrt{2}/3}{-1/3}=2\sqrt{2}.$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Verify that the identity $\cot^2(2\theta)+1=\csc^2(2\theta)$ holds for your values.
parts.markscheme:
- LHS $=\cot^2(2\theta)+1=(\tfrac{1}{2\sqrt{2}})^2+1=\tfrac{1}{8}+1=\tfrac{9}{8}.$ <marks>M1</marks>
- RHS $=\csc^2(2\theta)=\dfrac{1}{\sin^2(2\theta)}=\dfrac{1}{(-2\sqrt{2}/3)^2}=\dfrac{1}{8/9}=\tfrac{9}{8}.$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content:
A rotating beacon at $P$ emits a light beam of intensity $I$ proportional to $(\sin\theta+\cos\theta)^2$.
Determine the **maximum** and **minimum** possible intensities, giving exact values.
parts.markscheme:
- Expand: $(\sin\theta+\cos\theta)^2=1+\sin(2\theta).$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Max when $\sin(2\theta)=1\Rightarrow I_\text{max}=2.$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Min when $\sin(2\theta)=-1\Rightarrow I_\text{min}=0.$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Hence find the range of $\theta$ (in radians, $0\le\theta<2\pi$) for which the intensity is less than 1.
parts.markscheme:
- $1+\sin(2\theta)<1\Rightarrow \sin(2\theta)<0.$ <marks>M1</marks>
- $\sin(2\theta)=0\Rightarrow 2\theta=\pi,\,2\pi,\,3\pi.$
- Therefore $2\theta\in(\pi,2\pi)\cup(3\pi,4\pi)$. <marks>M1</marks>
- Divide by 2: $\theta\in\Bigl(\tfrac{\pi}{2},\pi\Bigr)\cup\Bigl(\tfrac{3\pi}{2},2\pi\Bigr).$ <marks>A1</marks>
