parts.content: Find all possible values of $\sin\theta$.
parts.markscheme:
From identity $\sin^2\theta+\cos^2\theta=1 \Rightarrow \sin^2\theta=1-\left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}\right)^2=1-\frac{3}{9}=\frac{6}{9}=\frac{2}{3}$. <marks>M1</marks>
$\sin\theta=\pm\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}=\pm\frac{\sqrt{6}}{3}$. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify known values
steps.content:
The coordinates of point $P$ on the unit circle are given as $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$. The question states that the $x$-coordinate is $-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$. Therefore, we can identify:

$$\cos\theta = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$$

steps.title: Apply Pythagorean identity
steps.content: To find $\sin\theta$ when $\cos\theta$ is known, we use the Pythagorean identity found in the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet.

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Substitute $\cos\theta = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$ into the identity and solve for $\sin^2\theta$:

$$\begin{aligned} \sin^2\theta + \left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}\right)^2 &= 1 \\ \sin^2\theta + \frac{3}{9} &= 1 \\ \sin^2\theta &= 1 - \frac{1}{3} \\ \sin^2\theta &= \frac{2}{3} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 1 minus the square of the cosine value

ti84.instructions: Calculate 1 minus the square of the cosine value

desmos.instructions: Calculate 1 minus the square of the cosine value

steps.title: Find final values
steps.content:
To find $\sin\theta$, take the square root of both sides. Since the question asks for *all possible values*, we must consider both positive and negative roots:

$$\sin\theta = \pm\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}$$

Rationalizing the denominator gives:

$$\sin\theta = \pm\frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}} = \pm\frac{\sqrt{6}}{3}$$

steps.title: Identify the trigonometric ratio
steps.content:
The question states that point $P$ has coordinates $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ on the unit circle. We are given the $x$-coordinate, which corresponds to $\cos\theta$.

$$\cos\theta = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$$

steps.title: Construct a reference triangle
steps.content:
To find the value of $\sin\theta$, we first construct a right-angled reference triangle. We ignore the negative sign for now and use the magnitude of cosine.

Using the definition $$\cos\theta = \frac{\text{Adjacent}}{\text{Hypotenuse}}$$ we can set:
- $\text{Adjacent} = \sqrt{3}$
- $\text{Hypotenuse} = 3$

steps.title: Calculate the opposite side
steps.content:
We use the Pythagorean theorem ($a^2 + b^2 = c^2$) to find the length of the opposite side. 

$$\begin{aligned} (\text{Opposite})^2 + (\text{Adjacent})^2 &= (\text{Hypotenuse})^2 \\ (\text{Opposite})^2 + (\sqrt{3})^2 &= 3^2 \\ (\text{Opposite})^2 + 3 &= 9 \\ (\text{Opposite})^2 &= 6 \\ \text{Opposite} &= \sqrt{6} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Verify the arithmetic for the squared terms

steps.title: Determine the magnitude of sine
steps.content:
Now we calculate the magnitude of $\sin\theta$ using the definition $$\sin\theta = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Hypotenuse}}$$

Substituting our values:
$$|\sin\theta| = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{3}$$

steps.title: Determine signs based on quadrants
steps.content:
The original value $\cos\theta = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$ is negative. Cosine is negative in the **2nd Quadrant** and **3rd Quadrant**.

- In the **2nd Quadrant**, sine is positive.
- In the **3rd Quadrant**, sine is negative.

Therefore, $\sin\theta$ can be either positive or negative.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Combining the magnitude with the possible signs:

$$\sin\theta = \pm\frac{\sqrt{6}}{3}$$

parts.content: If $P$ lies in the second quadrant, find the exact values of $\sin(2\theta)$, $\cos(2\theta)$, and $\tan(2\theta)$.
parts.markscheme:
In quadrant II: $\sin\theta=\frac{\sqrt{6}}{3}, \, \cos\theta=-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$. <marks>M1</marks>
$\sin(2\theta)=2\sin\theta\cos\theta=2\left(\frac{\sqrt{6}}{3}\right)\left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}\right)=-\frac{2\sqrt{18}}{9}=-\frac{6\sqrt{2}}{9}=-\frac{2\sqrt{2}}{3}$. <marks>A1</marks>
$\cos(2\theta)=2\cos^2\theta-1=2\left(\frac{3}{9}\right)-1=2\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)-1=-\frac{1}{3}$. <marks>A1</marks>
$\tan(2\theta)=\dfrac{\sin(2\theta)}{\cos(2\theta)}=\dfrac{-2\sqrt{2}/3}{-1/3}=2\sqrt{2}$. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
The problem states that point $P$ has coordinates $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$. Since the $x$-coordinate is given as $-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$, we know that $\cos\theta = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$.

We are also told that $P$ lies in the second quadrant, which means $\sin\theta$ must be positive.

steps.title: Find sine using identity
steps.content:
We use the Pythagorean identity from the data booklet to find $\sin\theta$. 

$$\cos^{2}\theta+\sin^{2}\theta=1$$

Substituting $\cos\theta = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}\right)^2 + \sin^2\theta &= 1 \\ \frac{3}{9} + \sin^2\theta &= 1 \\ \frac{1}{3} + \sin^2\theta &= 1 \\ \sin^2\theta &= \frac{2}{3} \end{aligned}$$

Since we are in the second quadrant, $\sin\theta$ is positive:

$$\sin\theta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{3}$$

calculator.description: Calculate 1 - (sqrt(3)/3)^2 to verify the value of sin^2(theta)

steps.title: Calculate sin(2θ)
steps.content:
Now we use the double angle formula for sine found in the data booklet.

$$\sin 2\theta=2\sin\theta\cos\theta$$

Substitute $\sin\theta = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{3}$ and $\cos\theta = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \sin(2\theta) &= 2\left(\frac{\sqrt{6}}{3}\right)\left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}\right) \\ &= -\frac{2\sqrt{18}}{9} \\ &= -\frac{2(3\sqrt{2})}{9} \\ &= -\frac{6\sqrt{2}}{9} \\ &= -\frac{2\sqrt{2}}{3} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate cos(2θ)
steps.content:
Next, we use the double angle formula for cosine.

$$\cos 2\theta=\cos^{2}\theta-\sin^{2}\theta$$

Using our values $\cos^2\theta = \frac{1}{3}$ and $\sin^2\theta = \frac{2}{3}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \cos(2\theta) &= \frac{1}{3} - \frac{2}{3} \\ &= -\frac{1}{3} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate tan(2θ)
steps.content:
Finally, find $\tan(2\theta)$ using the ratio identity $\tan A = \frac{\sin A}{\cos A}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \tan(2\theta) &= \frac{\sin(2\theta)}{\cos(2\theta)} \\ &= \frac{-\frac{2\sqrt{2}}{3}}{-\frac{1}{3}} \\ &= -\frac{2\sqrt{2}}{3} \times \left(-\frac{3}{1}\right) \\ &= 2\sqrt{2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Analyze the given information
steps.content:
We are given that point $P$ has an $x$-coordinate of $-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$. Since $P(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$, this means $\cos\theta = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$.

We are also told that $P$ is in the second quadrant. In this quadrant, $x$ ($\cos\theta$) is negative and $y$ ($\sin\theta$) is positive.

steps.title: Setup reference triangle
steps.content:
To find $\sin\theta$, we use the reference triangle method. We interpret $\cos\theta = \frac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = \frac{-\sqrt{3}}{3}$.

Imagine a right-angled triangle in the second quadrant with:
- Adjacent side ($x$) = $-\sqrt{3}$
- Hypotenuse ($r$) = $3$

We need to find the opposite side ($y$).

steps.title: Calculate the opposite side
steps.content:
Using the Pythagorean theorem $x^2 + y^2 = r^2$, we solve for $y$:

$$\begin{aligned} (-\sqrt{3})^2 + y^2 &= 3^2 \\ 3 + y^2 &= 9 \\ y^2 &= 6 \\ y &= \pm\sqrt{6} \end{aligned}$$

Since $P$ is in the second quadrant, the $y$-coordinate must be positive, so $y = \sqrt{6}$.

steps.title: State trigonometric ratios
steps.content:
Now we can write the sine and cosine ratios from our triangle:

$$\begin{aligned} \sin\theta &= \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{3} \\ \cos\theta &= \frac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find sin(2θ)
steps.content:
We use the double angle formula from the data booklet to find $\sin(2\theta)$.

$$\sin(2\theta) = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} \sin(2\theta) &= 2\left(\frac{\sqrt{6}}{3}\right)\left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}\right) \\ &= -\frac{2\sqrt{18}}{9} \\ &= -\frac{2(3\sqrt{2})}{9} \\ &= -\frac{6\sqrt{2}}{9} \\ &= -\frac{2\sqrt{2}}{3} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find cos(2θ)
steps.content:
Next, we use the double angle formula for cosine. While there are three versions, the data booklet provides $\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta$.

$$\begin{aligned} \cos(2\theta) &= \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta \\ &= \left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{\sqrt{6}}{3}\right)^2 \\ &= \frac{3}{9} - \frac{6}{9} \\ &= -\frac{3}{9} \\ &= -\frac{1}{3} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find tan(2θ)
steps.content:
Finally, we find $\tan(2\theta)$ using the ratio of sine and cosine:

$$\begin{aligned} \tan(2\theta) &= \frac{\sin(2\theta)}{\cos(2\theta)} \\ &= \frac{-\frac{2\sqrt{2}}{3}}{-\frac{1}{3}} \\ &= -\frac{2\sqrt{2}}{3} \times -\frac{3}{1} \\ &= 2\sqrt{2} \end{aligned}$$

parts.content: Verify that the identity $\cot^2(2\theta)+1=\csc^2(2\theta)$ holds for your values.
parts.markscheme:
LHS $=\cot^2(2\theta)+1=\left(\frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}}\right)^2+1=\frac{1}{8}+1=\frac{9}{8}$. <marks>M1</marks>
RHS $=\csc^2(2\theta)=\dfrac{1}{\sin^2(2\theta)}=\dfrac{1}{(-2\sqrt{2}/3)^2}=\dfrac{1}{8/9}=\frac{9}{8}$. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Find the values of sin²θ and cos²θ
steps.content:
First, we identify the values of $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ using the coordinates of point $P(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$.

From the question, we are given the $x$-coordinate:
$$\cos\theta = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$$

Using the Pythagorean identity $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$, we can find $\sin^2\theta$:
$$\begin{aligned} \left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}\right)^2 + \sin^2\theta &= 1 \\ \frac{3}{9} + \sin^2\theta &= 1 \\ \frac{1}{3} + \sin^2\theta &= 1 \\ \sin^2\theta &= \frac{2}{3} \end{aligned}$$

So, $\sin\theta = \pm\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}$. The sign depends on the quadrant, but since we will be squaring our final terms, we can carry the $\pm$.

steps.title: Calculate cos(2θ) and sin(2θ)
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the specific values for the double angles using the formulas from the data booklet.

For $\cos(2\theta)$:
$$\begin{aligned} \cos(2\theta) &= \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta \\ &= \frac{1}{3} - \frac{2}{3} \\ &= -\frac{1}{3} \end{aligned}$$

For $\sin(2\theta)$:
$$\begin{aligned} \sin(2\theta) &= 2\sin\theta\cos\theta \\ &= 2\left(\pm\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}\right)\left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}\right) \\ &= \mp \frac{2\sqrt{6}}{3\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}} \quad \text{(simplify surds)} \\ &= \mp \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{3} \end{aligned}$$

Squaring this gives us a unique value: $\sin^2(2\theta) = \frac{8}{9}$.

steps.title: Evaluate the Left Hand Side (LHS)
steps.content:
Now we substitute these values into the left side of the identity: $\cot^2(2\theta) + 1$.

Recall that $\cot(x) = \frac{\cos(x)}{\sin(x)}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \cot(2\theta) &= \frac{\cos(2\theta)}{\sin(2\theta)} \\ &= \frac{-1/3}{\mp 2\sqrt{2}/3} \\ &= \pm \frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}} \end{aligned}$$

Now square it and add 1:
$$\begin{aligned} \cot^2(2\theta) + 1 &= \left(\pm \frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 + 1 \\ &= \frac{1}{4(2)} + 1 \\ &= \frac{1}{8} + 1 \\ &= \frac{9}{8} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the value of (1/(2*sqrt(2)))^2 + 1 to confirm the fraction.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the value of (1/(2*sqrt(2)))^2 + 1 to confirm the fraction.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to verify the result

steps.title: Evaluate the Right Hand Side (RHS)
steps.content:
Finally, we substitute our value for $\sin(2\theta)$ into the right side: $\csc^2(2\theta)$.

Recall that $\csc(x) = \frac{1}{\sin(x)}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \csc^2(2\theta) &= \frac{1}{\sin^2(2\theta)} \\ &= \frac{1}{(8/9)} \\ &= \frac{9}{8} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content:
Since both sides equate to the same value, the identity is verified for our specific values.

$$ \text{LHS} = \frac{9}{8} = \text{RHS} $$

steps.title: Determine sine and cosine values
steps.content:
First, we identify the values of $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ from the given point coordinates. 

We are given the x-coordinate:
$$\cos\theta = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$$

Using the Pythagorean identity $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$, we find $\sin^2\theta$:
$$\begin{aligned} \sin^2\theta &= 1 - \cos^2\theta \\ &= 1 - \left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}\right)^2 \\ &= 1 - \frac{3}{9} \\ &= 1 - \frac{1}{3} \\ &= \frac{2}{3} \end{aligned}$$

So, $\cos^2\theta = \frac{1}{3}$ and $\sin^2\theta = \frac{2}{3}$.

steps.title: Evaluate the Right Hand Side (RHS)
steps.content:
We need to verify the identity by evaluating both sides separately. Let's start with the RHS, $\csc^2(2\theta)$.

Using the double angle formula for sine, substitute $\sin(2\theta) = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{RHS} &= \csc^2(2\theta) \\ &= \frac{1}{\sin^2(2\theta)} \\ &= \frac{1}{(2\sin\theta\cos\theta)^2} \\ &= \frac{1}{4\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta} \end{aligned}$$

Now, substitute our values $\sin^2\theta = \frac{2}{3}$ and $\cos^2\theta = \frac{1}{3}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{RHS} &= \frac{1}{4\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)} \\ &= \frac{1}{\frac{8}{9}} \\ &= \frac{9}{8} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Evaluate the Left Hand Side (LHS)
steps.content:
Next, we evaluate the LHS term $\cot^2(2\theta)$ using the double angle formulas. We express $\cot(2\theta)$ as $\frac{\cos(2\theta)}{\sin(2\theta)}$.

Using $\cos(2\theta) = \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta$:

$$\begin{aligned} \cot(2\theta) &= \frac{\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta}{2\sin\theta\cos\theta} \\ \cot^2(2\theta) &= \left(\frac{\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta}{2\sin\theta\cos\theta}\right)^2 \\ &= \frac{(\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta)^2}{4\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta} \end{aligned}$$

Substitute the values $\cos^2\theta = \frac{1}{3}$ and $\sin^2\theta = \frac{2}{3}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \cot^2(2\theta) &= \frac{(\frac{1}{3} - \frac{2}{3})^2}{\frac{8}{9}} \\ &= \frac{(-\frac{1}{3})^2}{\frac{8}{9}} \\ &= \frac{\frac{1}{9}}{\frac{8}{9}} \\ &= \frac{1}{8} \end{aligned}$$

Finally, add 1 to complete the LHS:
$$\text{LHS} = \cot^2(2\theta) + 1 = \frac{1}{8} + 1 = \frac{9}{8}$$

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content:
Comparing our results:

$$\text{LHS} = \frac{9}{8} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{RHS} = \frac{9}{8}$$

Since LHS = RHS, we have verified that the identity $\cot^2(2\theta)+1=\csc^2(2\theta)$ holds for these values.

steps.title: Find the angle θ
steps.content:
First, we determine the value of $\theta$ from the given $x$-coordinate. The question states the point has coordinates $P(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ and the $x$-coordinate is $-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$.

Using the inverse cosine function:

$$\theta = \arccos\left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}\right) \approx 2.1863 \text{ rad}$$

steps.title: Evaluate the Left Hand Side (LHS)
steps.content:
Now we substitute this value of $\theta$ into the left side of the identity: $\cot^2(2\theta)+1$.

Since most calculators do not have a dedicated cotangent button, we use the reciprocal identity $\cot(x) = \frac{1}{\tan(x)}$.

$$ \text{LHS} = \left(\frac{1}{\tan(2\theta)}\right)^2 + 1 = 1.125 $$

ti84.instructions: Use the previous answer (Ans): ( 1 / tan( 2 * Ans ) )^2 + 1

calculator.description: Evaluate LHS using the stored answer

steps.title: Evaluate the Right Hand Side (RHS)
steps.content:
Next, we evaluate the right side of the identity: $\csc^2(2\theta)$.

Using the reciprocal identity $\csc(x) = \frac{1}{\sin(x)}$:

$$ \text{RHS} = \left(\frac{1}{\sin(2\theta)}\right)^2 = 1.125 $$

ti84.instructions: Recall the angle value or use the original expression: ( 1 / sin( 2 * acos(-√(3)/3) ) )^2

steps.title: Verify equality
steps.content:
Finally, we compare the numerical results.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{LHS} &= 1.125 \\ \text{RHS} &= 1.125 \end{aligned}$$

Since both sides evaluate to the same value (which corresponds to the exact fraction $\frac{9}{8}$ found in the markscheme), the identity is verified.

parts.content: Show that $(\sin\theta+\cos\theta)^2=1+\sin(2\theta)$ and hence find the maximum and minimum values of $(\sin\theta+\cos\theta)^2$.
parts.markscheme:
Expand: $(\sin\theta+\cos\theta)^2=1+\sin(2\theta)$. <marks>M1</marks>
Max when $\sin(2\theta)=1 \Rightarrow I_{\text{max}}=2$. <marks>A1</marks>
Min when $\sin(2\theta)=-1 \Rightarrow I_{\text{min}}=0$. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Expand the expression
steps.content:
First, we expand the squared term using the algebraic identity $(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$. 

$$\begin{aligned} (\sin\theta + \cos\theta)^2 &= \sin^2\theta + 2\sin\theta\cos\theta + \cos^2\theta \\ &= (\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta) + 2\sin\theta\cos\theta \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Apply trigonometric identities
steps.content:
We simplify the expression using two identities from the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet:
1. The Pythagorean identity: $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$
2. The double angle identity: $\sin 2\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$

Substituting these into our expanded equation:

$$(\sin\theta + \cos\theta)^2 = 1 + \sin(2\theta)$$

This completes the "show that" part of the question.

steps.title: Determine the maximum value
steps.content:
To find the maximum value, we consider the range of the sine function. We know that for any real angle, the sine function varies between $-1$ and $1$:

$$-1 \le \sin(2\theta) \le 1$$

The maximum value of the expression occurs when $\sin(2\theta) = 1$.

$$\text{Maximum} = 1 + 1 = 2$$

steps.title: Determine the minimum value
steps.content:
Similarly, the minimum value of the expression occurs when $\sin(2\theta)$ is at its minimum value of $-1$.

$$\text{Minimum} = 1 + (-1) = 0$$

Therefore, the maximum value is $2$ and the minimum value is $0$.

steps.title: Expand the expression
steps.content:
First, we expand the squared expression $(\sin\theta + \cos\theta)^2$ using the algebraic identity $(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$:

$$(\sin\theta + \cos\theta)^2 = \sin^2\theta + 2\sin\theta\cos\theta + \cos^2\theta$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the left side and right side to verify they match.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to verify the value.

calculator.description: Verify the expansion numerically for a test angle, e.g., 30 degrees

steps.title: Apply trigonometric identities
steps.content:
We can rearrange the terms to group $\sin^2\theta$ and $\cos^2\theta$ together:

$$(\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta) + (2\sin\theta\cos\theta)$$

Now, we substitute the standard Pythagorean identity and the double angle identity from the data booklet:

$$1 + \sin(2\theta)$$

This shows the required result.

steps.title: Differentiate the function
steps.content:
To find the maximum and minimum values using the calculus approach, we treat the expression as a function of $\theta$:

$$f(\theta) = 1 + \sin(2\theta)$$

We find the derivative $f'(\theta)$ using the chain rule. The derivative of $\sin(u)$ is $\cos(u) \cdot u'$, where $u = 2\theta$ and $u' = 2$:

$$f'(\theta) = 0 + \cos(2\theta) \times 2$$
$$f'(\theta) = 2\cos(2\theta)$$

steps.title: Find critical points
steps.content:
We find the critical points by setting the derivative to zero:

$$2\cos(2\theta) = 0$$
$$\cos(2\theta) = 0$$

For $\cos(2\theta) = 0$, the angle $2\theta$ corresponds to points on the vertical axis of the unit circle (like $\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3\pi}{2}$, etc.).

gdc.instructions: Graph y = 2cos(2x) and find the zeroes.

ti84.instructions: Graph Y1 = 2cos(2X) and find the roots.

desmos.instructions: Graph the derivative to see zero crossings.

calculator.description: Visualize the derivative function to see where it crosses zero

steps.title: Evaluate extrema
steps.content:
At these critical points where $\cos(2\theta) = 0$, the Pythagorean identity $\sin^2(2\theta) + \cos^2(2\theta) = 1$ implies that $\sin^2(2\theta) = 1$, so $\sin(2\theta)$ must be either $1$ or $-1$.

We substitute these values back into our original function $f(\theta) = 1 + \sin(2\theta)$:

1.  **Maximum:** When $\sin(2\theta) = 1$:
    $$f_{\text{max}} = 1 + 1 = 2$$

2.  **Minimum:** When $\sin(2\theta) = -1$:
    $$f_{\text{min}} = 1 + (-1) = 0$$

**Final Answer:**
Maximum value: **2**
Minimum value: **0**

parts.content: Find the values of $\theta$ in the interval $0 \le \theta \le 2\pi$ for which $(\sin\theta+\cos\theta)^2 < 1$.
parts.markscheme:
$1+\sin(2\theta)<1 \Rightarrow \sin(2\theta)<0$. <marks>M1</marks>
$\sin(2\theta)=0 \Rightarrow 2\theta=\pi, \, 2\pi, \, 3\pi$.
Therefore $2\theta \in (\pi, 2\pi) \cup (3\pi, 4\pi)$. <marks>M1</marks>
Divide by 2: $\theta \in \left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right) \cup \left(\frac{3\pi}{2}, 2\pi\right)$. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Expand the squared expression
steps.content:
First, we expand the expression on the left side of the inequality $(\sin\theta+\cos\theta)^2 < 1$.

$$\begin{aligned} (\sin\theta+\cos\theta)^2 &= \sin^2\theta + 2\sin\theta\cos\theta + \cos^2\theta \\ &= (\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta) + 2\sin\theta\cos\theta \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Apply trigonometric identities
steps.content:
We simplify the expanded expression using two key identities from the data booklet:
1. The Pythagorean identity: $$\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$$
2. The double angle identity: $$\sin 2\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$$

Substituting these into our equation:
$$(\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta) + 2\sin\theta\cos\theta = 1 + \sin(2\theta)$$

steps.title: Set up the inequality
steps.content:
Now substitute the simplified form back into the original inequality $(\sin\theta+\cos\theta)^2 < 1$:

$$\begin{aligned} 1 + \sin(2\theta) &< 1 \\ \sin(2\theta) &< 0 \end{aligned}$$

We need to find values of $\theta$ where the sine of double the angle is negative.

steps.title: Determine the domain for 2θ
steps.content:
The question specifies the interval for $\theta$ is $0 \le \theta \le 2\pi$. Since our inequality involves $2\theta$, we must adjust the domain:

$$0 \le 2\theta \le 4\pi$$

This means we need to look for solutions over two full rotations of the unit circle.

steps.title: Solve for 2θ
steps.content:
We need to find where $\sin(A) < 0$. The sine function is negative in the 3rd and 4th quadrants (between $\pi$ and $2\pi$).

For the first rotation ($0$ to $2\pi$):
$$\pi < 2\theta < 2\pi$$

For the second rotation ($2\pi$ to $4\pi$):
$$3\pi < 2\theta < 4\pi$$

Note: We use strictly less than ($<$) because the original inequality is strict.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Graph menu
2. Enter y = sin(2x)
3. Set V-Window: x from 0 to 2π
4. Identify intervals where y < 0

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=]
2. Enter Y1 = sin(2X)
3. Press [WINDOW]
4. Set Xmin=0, Xmax=2π (approx 6.28), Ymin=-1.5, Ymax=1.5
5. Press [GRAPH]
6. Observe where the graph is below the x-axis

desmos.instructions:
1. Graph y = sin(2x)
2. Restrict domain: 0 <= x <= 2pi
3. Look for regions where y < 0

steps.title: Solve for θ
steps.content:
Finally, divide the inequalities by 2 to solve for $\theta$:

Interval 1:
$$\begin{aligned} \pi &< 2\theta < 2\pi \\ \frac{\pi}{2} &< \theta < \pi \end{aligned}$$

Interval 2:
$$\begin{aligned} 3\pi &< 2\theta < 4\pi \\ \frac{3\pi}{2} &< \theta < 2\pi \end{aligned}$$

Combining these gives the final solution set:
$$\theta \in \left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right) \cup \left(\frac{3\pi}{2}, 2\pi\right)$$

steps.title: Expand the squared term
steps.content:
First, we expand the left-hand side of the inequality $(\sin\theta+\cos\theta)^2 < 1$. 

Using the algebraic expansion $(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$:

$$ \sin^2\theta + 2\sin\theta\cos\theta + \cos^2\theta < 1 $$

steps.title: Apply Pythagorean identity
steps.content:
We can simplify this expression using the Pythagorean identity from the data booklet. Since $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$, we substitute this into our inequality:

$$ 1 + 2\sin\theta\cos\theta < 1 $$

steps.title: Simplify the inequality
steps.content:
Subtract 1 from both sides to isolate the trigonometric term:

$$ 2\sin\theta\cos\theta < 0 $$

Dividing by 2 (which is positive and does not flip the inequality sign) gives us our condition for sign analysis:

$$ \sin\theta\cos\theta < 0 $$

steps.title: Analyze signs of factors
steps.content:
For the product of two numbers to be **negative** ($< 0$), the factors $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ must have **opposite signs**.

We examine the unit circle quadrants ($0 \le \theta \le 2\pi$):

*   **Quadrant I:** $\sin\theta > 0$, $\cos\theta > 0$ $\rightarrow$ Product is positive $(+)$.
*   **Quadrant II:** $\sin\theta > 0$, $\cos\theta < 0$ $\rightarrow$ Product is **negative** $(-)$.
*   **Quadrant III:** $\sin\theta < 0$, $\cos\theta < 0$ $\rightarrow$ Product is positive $(+)$.
*   **Quadrant IV:** $\sin\theta < 0$, $\cos\theta > 0$ $\rightarrow$ Product is **negative** $(-)$.

steps.title: Determine the intervals
steps.content:
The inequality holds true in **Quadrant II** and **Quadrant IV**. We write these regions as intervals within the given domain $0 \le \theta \le 2\pi$.

*   **Quadrant II:** $\frac{\pi}{2} < \theta < \pi$
*   **Quadrant IV:** $\frac{3\pi}{2} < \theta < 2\pi$

Note: The inequality is strict ($<$), so we do not include the boundaries where the product is zero.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression in calculation mode:

calculator.description: Verify a value in Quadrant II (e.g., 3pi/4) satisfies the original inequality

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The values of $\theta$ satisfying the inequality are:

$$ \theta \in \left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right) \cup \left(\frac{3\pi}{2}, 2\pi\right) $$

steps.title: Identify the method
steps.content: The problem asks us to solve the inequality $(\sin\theta+\cos\theta)^2 < 1$. We will use the **Harmonic Form Transformation** to combine the sine and cosine terms into a single trigonometric function of the form $R\sin(\theta + \alpha)$.

steps.title: Convert to Harmonic Form
steps.content:
We express $\sin\theta + \cos\theta$ in the form $R\sin(\theta + \alpha)$. Comparing coefficients $1\sin\theta + 1\cos\theta$ with the compound angle expansion:

$$R\sin(\theta + \alpha) = R(\sin\theta\cos\alpha + \cos\theta\sin\alpha)$$

We calculate $R$ and $\alpha$:
$$\begin{aligned} R &= \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{2} \\ \tan\alpha &= \frac{1}{1} = 1 \Rightarrow \alpha = \frac{\pi}{4} \end{aligned}$$

So, the expression becomes:
$$\sin\theta + \cos\theta = \sqrt{2}\sin\left(\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$

steps.title: Substitute into the inequality
steps.content:
Now we substitute this back into the original inequality $(\sin\theta+\cos\theta)^2 < 1$:

$$\begin{aligned} \left(\sqrt{2}\sin\left(\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right)^2 &< 1 \\ 2\sin^2\left(\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) &< 1 \\ \sin^2\left(\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) &< \frac{1}{2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for the single angle
steps.content:
Taking the square root of both sides, we are looking for where the magnitude of the sine is less than $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} < \sin\left(\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) < \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Let $u = \theta + \frac{\pi}{4}$. The inequality becomes:
$$-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} < \sin u < \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$ 

We must also adjust our domain. Since $0 \le \theta \le 2\pi$, the range for $u$ is:
$$\frac{\pi}{4} \le u \le 2\pi + \frac{\pi}{4}$$

steps.title: Find valid intervals for u
steps.content:
The critical values where $\sin u = \pm\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ are multiples of $\frac{\pi}{4}$. Checking the regions within the domain $\left[\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{9\pi}{4}\right]$:

1.  **$\frac{\pi}{4}$ to $\frac{3\pi}{4}$**: sine values are $> \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (e.g., at $u=\frac{\pi}{2}$, $\sin u = 1$). **Reject.**
2.  **$\frac{3\pi}{4}$ to $\frac{5\pi}{4}$**: sine values drop from $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ down to $-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$, passing through 0. **Accept.**
3.  **$\frac{5\pi}{4}$ to $\frac{7\pi}{4}$**: sine values are $< -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (e.g., at $u=\frac{3\pi}{2}$, $\sin u = -1$). **Reject.**
4.  **$\frac{7\pi}{4}$ to $\frac{9\pi}{4}$**: sine values rise from $-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$, passing through 0. **Accept.**

So, the valid intervals for $u$ are:
$$u \in \left(\frac{3\pi}{4}, \frac{5\pi}{4}\right) \cup \left(\frac{7\pi}{4}, \frac{9\pi}{4}\right)$$

steps.title: Convert back to theta
steps.content:
Finally, substitute $u = \theta + \frac{\pi}{4}$ back to solve for $\theta$.

For the first interval:
$$\begin{aligned} \frac{3\pi}{4} &< \theta + \frac{\pi}{4} < \frac{5\pi}{4} \\ \frac{2\pi}{4} &< \theta < \frac{4\pi}{4} \\ \frac{\pi}{2} &< \theta < \pi \end{aligned}$$

For the second interval:
$$\begin{aligned} \frac{7\pi}{4} &< \theta + \frac{\pi}{4} < \frac{9\pi}{4} \\ \frac{6\pi}{4} &< \theta < \frac{8\pi}{4} \\ \frac{3\pi}{2} &< \theta < 2\pi \end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\theta \in \left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right) \cup \left(\frac{3\pi}{2}, 2\pi\right)$$

questions.specification:
A point \(P\) moves around the unit circle centered at the origin \(O(0,0)\) with coordinates \(P(\cos\theta,\sin\theta)\).

At a certain instant, the \(y\)-coordinate of \(P\) is \(-\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\).
Find all possible values of \(\cos\theta\).

parts.content: Find all possible values of \(\cos\theta\).
parts.markscheme:
- Use identity \(\sin^2\theta+\cos^2\theta=1\Rightarrow \cos^2\theta=1-(-\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}})^2=\tfrac{4}{5}\). <marks>M1</marks>
- State the values: \(\cos\theta=\pm\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{5}}\). <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify sin θ from coordinates
steps.content:
The problem states that point $P$ has coordinates $P(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$. The $y$-coordinate corresponds to $\sin\theta$.

Since the question gives the $y$-coordinate as $-\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$, we identify:

$$\sin\theta = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$$ 

This concept relates to **Topic 3.5: Unit circle definitions**.

steps.title: Use the Pythagorean Identity
steps.content:
To find $\cos\theta$ when $\sin\theta$ is known, we use the fundamental Pythagorean Identity found in the **Trigonometry** section of your data booklet.

Substituting $\sin\theta = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$ into the identity:

$$\cos^2\theta + \left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\right)^2 = 1$$

steps.title: Solve for cos² θ
steps.content:
First, square the value of $\sin\theta$:

$$\left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{5}$$

Now, rearrange the equation to solve for $\cos^2\theta$:

$$\begin{aligned} \cos^2\theta + \frac{1}{5} &= 1 \\ \cos^2\theta &= 1 - \frac{1}{5} \\ \cos^2\theta &= \frac{4}{5} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the value of 1 minus the square of the y-coordinate

steps.title: Find the values of cos θ
steps.content:
Take the square root of both sides to find $\cos\theta$. Remember to include both the positive and negative roots ($\,\pm\,$) because the angle $\theta$ could be in Quadrant 3 (where cosine is negative) or Quadrant 4 (where cosine is positive).

$$\cos\theta = \pm\sqrt{\frac{4}{5}}$$

Simplify the result:

$$\cos\theta = \pm\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}$$ 

This matches the markscheme requirement to state the values.

steps.title: Identify sine from coordinates
steps.content:
The coordinates of point $P$ on the unit circle are given as $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$. The question states that the $y$-coordinate corresponds to $\sin\theta$. 

Therefore:
$$\sin\theta = -\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$$ 

We need to find the possible values of the $x$-coordinate, which corresponds to $\cos\theta$.

steps.title: Set up reference triangle
steps.content:
To find the magnitude of $\cos\theta$, we use a **reference triangle**. We ignore the negative sign for a moment and consider a right-angled triangle where the sine ratio is $\frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$.

*   **Opposite side** $= 1$
*   **Hypotenuse** $= \sqrt{5}$

steps.title: Find the adjacent side
steps.content:
We use the Pythagorean theorem to find the adjacent side ($a$):

$$\begin{aligned} a^2 + \text{opp}^2 &= \text{hyp}^2 \\ a^2 + 1^2 &= (\sqrt{5})^2 \\ a^2 + 1 &= 5 \\ a^2 &= 4 \\ a &= 2 \end{aligned}$$

So, the adjacent side is $2$. Using the definition $\cos\theta = \frac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$, the magnitude of cosine is $\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}$.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the square of the hypotenuse minus the square of the opposite side.

steps.title: Determine signs using quadrants
steps.content:
Now we determine the correct signs. We know $\sin\theta$ is negative ($y < 0$). On the unit circle, the $y$-coordinate is negative in **Quadrant 3** and **Quadrant 4**.

*   **In Quadrant 3:** The $x$-coordinate (cosine) is negative.
*   **In Quadrant 4:** The $x$-coordinate (cosine) is positive.

Since both quadrants are possible, $\cos\theta$ can be either positive or negative.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Combining the magnitude from our triangle with the possible signs from the quadrants, the possible values for $\cos\theta$ are:

$$\cos\theta = \pm\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{5}}$$

parts.content: If \(P\) lies in the fourth quadrant, find the exact values of \(\sin(2\theta)\), \(\cos(2\theta)\), and \(\tan(2\theta)\).
parts.markscheme:
- Identify quadrant IV signs: \(\sin\theta=-\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}},\,\cos\theta=\tfrac{2}{\sqrt{5}}\). <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate sine double angle: \(\sin(2\theta)=2\sin\theta\cos\theta=2(-\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}})(\tfrac{2}{\sqrt{5}})=-\tfrac{4}{5}.\) <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate cosine double angle: \(\cos(2\theta)=2\cos^2\theta-1=2(\tfrac{4}{5})-1=\tfrac{3}{5}.\) <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate tangent double angle: \(\tan(2\theta)=\dfrac{\sin(2\theta)}{\cos(2\theta)}=\dfrac{-4/5}{3/5}=-\dfrac{4}{3}.\) <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Analyze given information
steps.content:
From the question, we know the $y$-coordinate of point $P$ corresponds to $\sin\theta$. We are given:

$$\sin\theta = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$$

We are also told that point $P$ lies in the **fourth quadrant**. In the fourth quadrant, the $x$-coordinate ($\cos\theta$) is positive, while the $y$-coordinate ($\sin\theta$) is negative.

steps.title: Find cosine using identity
steps.content:
To find $\cos\theta$, we use the Pythagorean identity from the data booklet:

$$\cos^{2}\theta+\sin^{2}\theta=1$$

Substituting $\sin\theta = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \cos^2\theta + \left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\right)^2 &= 1 \\ \cos^2\theta + \frac{1}{5} &= 1 \\ \cos^2\theta &= 1 - \frac{1}{5} \\ \cos^2\theta &= \frac{4}{5} \end{aligned}$$

Since we are in the fourth quadrant, $\cos\theta$ must be positive:

$$\cos\theta = \sqrt{\frac{4}{5}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}$$.

steps.title: Calculate sin(2θ)
steps.content:
Now we find $\sin(2\theta)$ using the double angle formula from the data booklet:

$$\sin 2\theta=2\sin\theta\cos\theta$$

Substitute $\sin\theta = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$ and $\cos\theta = \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \sin(2\theta) &= 2\left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\right)\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}\right) \\ &= 2\left(-\frac{2}{5}\right) \\ &= -\frac{4}{5} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate cos(2θ)
steps.content:
Next, we find $\cos(2\theta)$ using the double angle formula from the data booklet:

$$\cos 2\theta=\cos^{2}\theta-\sin^{2}\theta$$

Substitute our squared values $\cos^2\theta = \frac{4}{5}$ and $\sin^2\theta = \frac{1}{5}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \cos(2\theta) &= \frac{4}{5} - \frac{1}{5} \\ &= \frac{3}{5} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate tan(2θ)
steps.content:
Finally, we find $\tan(2\theta)$ using the definition $\tan A = \frac{\sin A}{\cos A}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \tan(2\theta) &= \frac{\sin(2\theta)}{\cos(2\theta)} \\ &= \frac{-4/5}{3/5} \\ &= -\frac{4}{3} \end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\sin(2\theta) = -\frac{4}{5}, \quad \cos(2\theta) = \frac{3}{5}, \quad \tan(2\theta) = -\frac{4}{3}$$

steps.title: Setup the reference triangle
steps.content:
First, we interpret the given information using a reference triangle approach. We are given the $y$-coordinate, which corresponds to $\sin\theta$ on the unit circle:

$$\sin\theta = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$$

Since $\sin\theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$, we can sketch a right-angled triangle with:
*   $\text{Opposite} = -1$ (taking the sign into account for the coordinate)
*   $\text{Hypotenuse} = \sqrt{5}$

steps.title: Find the adjacent side
steps.content:
Using Pythagoras' theorem ($a^2 + b^2 = c^2$), we find the adjacent side length ($x$).

$$\begin{aligned} x^2 + (-1)^2 &= (\sqrt{5})^2 \\ x^2 + 1 &= 5 \\ x^2 &= 4 \\ x &= \pm 2 \end{aligned}$$

Since the question states $P$ lies in the **fourth quadrant**, the $x$-coordinate must be positive. Therefore, the adjacent side is $2$.

ti84.instructions: Calculate sqrt(5^2 - 1^2) to find the missing side length

calculator.description: Calculate the adjacent side using Pythagoras

steps.title: Determine cos theta
steps.content:
Now we can read the cosine ratio from our triangle using $\cos\theta = \frac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$.

With $\text{adjacent} = 2$ and $\text{hypotenuse} = \sqrt{5}$:

$$\cos\theta = \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}$$

Note that in the fourth quadrant, cosine is positive, which matches our result.

steps.title: Calculate sine double angle
steps.content:
To find $\sin(2\theta)$, we use the double angle identity from the data booklet.

$$ \sin(2\theta) = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta $$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} \sin(2\theta) &= 2\left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\right)\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}\right) \\ &= 2\left(-\frac{2}{5}\right) \\ &= -\frac{4}{5} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate cosine double angle
steps.content:
To find $\cos(2\theta)$, we use the double angle identity. You can use any of the forms, but we will use the primary one from the data booklet.

$$ \cos(2\theta) = \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta $$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} \cos(2\theta) &= \left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}\right)^2 - \left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\right)^2 \\ &= \frac{4}{5} - \frac{1}{5} \\ &= \frac{3}{5} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate tangent double angle
steps.content:
Finally, we find $\tan(2\theta)$ using the ratio identity $\tan A = \frac{\sin A}{\cos A}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \tan(2\theta) &= \frac{\sin(2\theta)}{\cos(2\theta)} \\ &= \frac{-4/5}{3/5} \\ &= -\frac{4}{3} \end{aligned}$$

parts.content: Verify that the identity \(1+\tan^2(2\theta)=\sec^2(2\theta)\) holds for your values.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute into LHS: \(1+\tan^2(2\theta)=1+(-\tfrac{4}{3})^2=1+\tfrac{16}{9}=\tfrac{25}{9}.\) <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute into RHS: \(\sec^2(2\theta)=\dfrac{1}{\cos^2(2\theta)}=\dfrac{1}{(3/5)^2}=\tfrac{25}{9}.\) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
From the question specification, we are given the $y$-coordinate of point $P$, which corresponds to $\sin\theta$:

$$\sin\theta = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$$ 

Using the Pythagorean identity $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$, we can find $\cos^2\theta$:

$$\begin{aligned} \cos^2\theta + \left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\right)^2 &= 1 \\ \cos^2\theta + \frac{1}{5} &= 1 \\ \cos^2\theta &= \frac{4}{5} \end{aligned}$$

So, $\cos\theta = \pm\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}$.

steps.title: Calculate sin(2θ)
steps.content:
We calculate $\sin(2\theta)$ using the double angle identity from the data booklet.

$$\sin(2\theta) = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$$

Substituting our values:

$$\sin(2\theta) = 2\left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\right)\left(\pm\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}\right) = \mp\frac{4}{5}$$

steps.title: Calculate cos(2θ)
steps.content:
Next, we calculate $\cos(2\theta)$ using the double angle identity. Note that the sign of $\cos\theta$ does not affect this result since we use squared terms.

$$\cos(2\theta) = \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta$$

Substituting our values:

$$\cos(2\theta) = \frac{4}{5} - \frac{1}{5} = \frac{3}{5}$$

steps.title: Determine tan(2θ)
steps.content:
Now we find $\tan(2\theta)$ as the ratio of sine to cosine:

$$\tan(2\theta) = \frac{\sin(2\theta)}{\cos(2\theta)} = \frac{\mp 4/5}{3/5} = \mp\frac{4}{3}$$

steps.title: Substitute into LHS
steps.content:
To verify the identity, we first substitute our value for $\tan(2\theta)$ into the Left Hand Side (LHS). Since we are squaring, the negative sign disappears:

$$\text{LHS} = 1 + \tan^2(2\theta) = 1 + \left(\mp\frac{4}{3}\right)^2$$

$$\text{LHS} = 1 + \frac{16}{9} = \frac{9}{9} + \frac{16}{9} = \frac{25}{9}$$

steps.title: Substitute into RHS
steps.content:
Next, we substitute our value for $\cos(2\theta)$ into the Right Hand Side (RHS). Recall that $\sec(x) = \frac{1}{\cos(x)}$.

$$\text{RHS} = \sec^2(2\theta) = \frac{1}{\cos^2(2\theta)} = \frac{1}{(3/5)^2}$$

$$\text{RHS} = \frac{1}{9/25} = \frac{25}{9}$$

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content:
We have shown that:

$$\text{LHS} = \frac{25}{9} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{RHS} = \frac{25}{9}$$

Since $\text{LHS} = \text{RHS}$, the identity $1+\tan^2(2\theta)=\sec^2(2\theta)$ is verified.

steps.title: Calculate tan(θ)
steps.content:
To evaluate the tangent double angle, we first need to find $\tan\theta$. Recall from previous parts that at point $P$, $\sin\theta = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$ and we found two possible values for $\cos\theta$: $\pm\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}$.

Using the definition $\tan\theta = \frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}$:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{-1/\sqrt{5}}{\pm 2/\sqrt{5}} = \mp \frac{1}{2}$$

Since we will square our result later, the sign does not affect the final value. We will proceed using $\tan\theta = -\frac{1}{2}$.

steps.title: Evaluate LHS with Tangent Double Angle
steps.content:
We need to verify the Left Hand Side (LHS) of the identity: $1+\tan^2(2\theta)$.

First, we use the double angle formula for tangent, $\tan(2\theta) = \frac{2\tan\theta}{1-\tan^2\theta}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \tan(2\theta) &= \frac{2(-1/2)}{1-(-1/2)^2} \\ &= \frac{-1}{1 - 1/4} \\ &= \frac{-1}{3/4} \\ &= -\frac{4}{3} \end{aligned}$$

Now substitute this into the LHS expression:

$$\text{LHS} = 1 + \left(-\frac{4}{3}\right)^2 = 1 + \frac{16}{9} = \frac{25}{9}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the value of the LHS expression

steps.title: Evaluate RHS with Cosine Double Angle
steps.content:
Now we evaluate the Right Hand Side (RHS): $\sec^2(2\theta) = \frac{1}{\cos^2(2\theta)}$.

First, find $\cos(2\theta)$ using the double angle formula from the data booklet. We know $\cos^2\theta = \frac{4}{5}$ and $\sin^2\theta = \frac{1}{5}$.

$$\cos 2\theta = \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta$$

Substituting our values:

$$\cos(2\theta) = \frac{4}{5} - \frac{1}{5} = \frac{3}{5}$$

Now substitute this into the RHS expression:

$$\text{RHS} = \frac{1}{(3/5)^2} = \frac{1}{9/25} = \frac{25}{9}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the value of the RHS expression

steps.title: Verify identity
steps.content:
Comparing our results:

$$\text{LHS} = \frac{25}{9}$$ 
$$\text{RHS} = \frac{25}{9}$$

Since $\text{LHS} = \text{RHS}$, the identity $1+\tan^2(2\theta)=\sec^2(2\theta)$ is verified.

parts.content:
A rotating sensor at \(P\) measures a quantity \(Q\) defined by \(Q = (\sqrt{5}\cos\theta-\sqrt{5}\sin\theta)^2\).

Determine the **maximum** and **minimum** possible values of \(Q\), giving exact values.
parts.markscheme:
- Simplify expression: \(Q = 5(\cos\theta-\sin\theta)^2 = 5(1-\sin(2\theta)).\) <marks>M1</marks>
- Identify max condition: Max when \(\sin(2\theta)=-1\Rightarrow Q_{\text{max}}=5(1-(-1))=10.\) <marks>A1</marks>
- Identify min condition: Min when \(\sin(2\theta)=1\Rightarrow Q_{\text{min}}=5(1-1)=0.\) <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Set up the expression
steps.content:
First, let's write down the expression for $Q$ and factor out the constant to make the algebra easier. We are given:

$$Q = (\sqrt{5}\cos\theta-\sqrt{5}\sin\theta)^2$$

We can factor out $\sqrt{5}$ inside the bracket:

$$Q = (\sqrt{5}(\cos\theta-\sin\theta))^2$$

Since $(\sqrt{5})^2 = 5$, this simplifies to:

$$Q = 5(\cos\theta-\sin\theta)^2$$

steps.title: Expand the squared term
steps.content:
Next, we expand the term $(\cos\theta-\sin\theta)^2$ using the algebraic identity $(a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$:

$$Q = 5(\cos^2\theta - 2\cos\theta\sin\theta + \sin^2\theta)$$

Rearranging the terms inside the bracket groups the squared trigonometric functions together:

$$Q = 5((\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) - 2\sin\theta\cos\theta)$$

steps.title: Apply trigonometric identities
steps.content:
Now we use two fundamental identities from the **Trigonometry** section of your data booklet to simplify the expression completely.

First, the Pythagorean identity:
$$\cos^{2}\theta+\sin^{2}\theta=1$$

Second, the double angle identity:
$$\sin 2\theta=2\sin\theta\cos\theta$$

Substituting these into our equation for $Q$:

$$Q = 5(1 - \sin 2\theta)$$

steps.title: Analyze the range
steps.content:
To find the maximum and minimum values of $Q$, we analyze the sine function. We know that for any real angle, the sine function oscillates between $-1$ and $1$:

$$-1 \le \sin 2\theta \le 1$$

We need to find the extreme values of $Q = 5(1 - \sin 2\theta)$.

steps.title: Verify with calculator
steps.content: Before finalizing the calculation, we can visualize the function $Q$ to confirm our logic. By graphing the function, we can clearly see the wave pattern and identify the highest and lowest points.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Graph menu
2. Enter y1 = (sqrt(5)cos(x) - sqrt(5)sin(x))^2
3. Set View Window to Trigonometry
4. Use G-Solv > Max to find the peak
5. Use G-Solv > Min to find the trough

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=]
2. Enter Y1 = (√(5)cos(X) - √(5)sin(X))^2
3. Press [ZOOM] > [7:ZTrig]
4. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC)
5. Select [3:minimum] to find the low point (should be 0)
6. Select [4:maximum] to find the high point (should be 10)

desmos.instructions:
1. Type f(x) = (sqrt(5)cos(x) - sqrt(5)sin(x))^2
2. Click on the wave peaks and troughs to see their y-coordinates

calculator.description: Graph the original function to visually verify the maximum and minimum y-values.

steps.title: Calculate maximum and minimum
steps.content:
Now we substitute the extreme values of $\sin 2\theta$ into our simplified equation $Q = 5(1 - \sin 2\theta)$.

**Maximum Value:**
$Q$ is maximized when the term being subtracted, $\sin 2\theta$, is at its minimum value ($-1$).
$$Q_{\text{max}} = 5(1 - (-1)) = 5(2) = 10$$

**Minimum Value:**
$Q$ is minimized when $\sin 2\theta$ is at its maximum value ($1$).
$$Q_{\text{min}} = 5(1 - 1) = 5(0) = 0$$

Therefore, the maximum value is $10$ and the minimum value is $0$.

steps.title: Factor out the constant
steps.content:
To simplify the expression for $Q$, we can first factor out $\sqrt{5}$ from the term being squared. This isolates the trigonometric expression $\cos\theta - \sin\theta$, which we will transform using the Harmonic Form.

$$\begin{aligned} Q &= (\sqrt{5}\cos\theta - \sqrt{5}\sin\theta)^2 \\ &= (\sqrt{5}(\cos\theta - \sin\theta))^2 \\ &= 5(\cos\theta - \sin\theta)^2 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Set up Harmonic Form
steps.content:
We transform the inner expression $\cos\theta - \sin\theta$ into the harmonic form $R\cos(\theta + \alpha)$. Using the compound angle identity for cosine, we can match coefficients.

$$\begin{aligned} R\cos(\theta + \alpha) &= R(\cos\theta\cos\alpha - \sin\theta\sin\alpha) \\ &= (R\cos\alpha)\cos\theta - (R\sin\alpha)\sin\theta \end{aligned}$$

Comparing this to $1\cos\theta - 1\sin\theta$, we get:
$$\begin{aligned} R\cos\alpha &= 1 \\ R\sin\alpha &= 1 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate R and α
steps.content:
Now we calculate the values of $R$ and $\alpha$:

**Finding $R$:**
$$R = \sqrt{(1)^2 + (1)^2} = \sqrt{2}$$

**Finding $\alpha$:**
$$\tan\alpha = \frac{R\sin\alpha}{R\cos\alpha} = \frac{1}{1} = 1$$

Since sine and cosine are both positive, $\alpha$ is in the first quadrant:
$$\alpha = \frac{\pi}{4}$$

So, the harmonic form is:
$$\cos\theta - \sin\theta = \sqrt{2}\cos\left(\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the magnitude R and the angle alpha

ti84.instructions: Calculate the magnitude R and the angle alpha

desmos.instructions: Calculate the magnitude R and the angle alpha

steps.title: Substitute back into Q
steps.content:
Substitute the harmonic form back into our simplified equation for $Q$ from Step 0.

$$\begin{aligned} Q &= 5(\cos\theta - \sin\theta)^2 \\ &= 5\left(\sqrt{2}\cos\left(\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right)^2 \\ &= 5 \cdot 2 \cos^2\left(\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) \\ &= 10\cos^2\left(\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine max and min
steps.content:
We analyze the range of the function $10\cos^2(\dots)$ to find the maximum and minimum values.

We know that for any real angle $x$, the range of $\cos x$ is $[-1, 1]$. Therefore, the range of $\cos^2 x$ is $[0, 1]$.

**Maximum Value:**
Occurs when $\cos^2\left(\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) = 1$.
$$Q_{\text{max}} = 10(1) = 10$$

**Minimum Value:**
Occurs when $\cos^2\left(\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) = 0$.
$$Q_{\text{min}} = 10(0) = 0$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Graph menu
2. Enter f1(x) = (sqrt(5)cos(x) - sqrt(5)sin(x))^2
3. Set View Window to x: [0, 2pi], y: [-1, 12]
4. Use G-Solve > Min and Max to verify values

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=]
2. Enter Y1 = (sqrt(5)cos(X) - sqrt(5)sin(X))^2
3. Press [WINDOW] and set Xmin=0, Xmax=2pi, Ymin=-1, Ymax=12
4. Press [GRAPH]
5. Use [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) > 3:Minimum and 4:Maximum to verify

desmos.instructions:
1. Type y = (\sqrt{5}\cos x - \sqrt{5}\sin x)^2
2. Observe the wave oscillates between y=0 and y=10

calculator.description: Visualize the function to confirm max and min

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content: The maximum possible value is **10** and the minimum possible value is **0**.

steps.title: Simplify the expression for Q
steps.content:
To make differentiation easier, we first expand and simplify the expression for $Q$. 

Starting with the given equation:
$$Q = (\sqrt{5}\cos\theta - \sqrt{5}\sin\theta)^2$$

Factor out $\sqrt{5}$:
$$Q = (\sqrt{5}(\cos\theta - \sin\theta))^2 = 5(\cos\theta - \sin\theta)^2$$

Expand the squared term:
$$Q = 5(\cos^2\theta - 2\sin\theta\cos\theta + \sin^2\theta)$$

Using the Pythagorean identity $\cos^2\theta+\sin^2\theta=1$ and the double angle identity $\sin 2\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$:
$$Q = 5(1 - \sin(2\theta))$$

steps.title: Differentiate Q with respect to θ
steps.content:
Now we apply the calculus method. We differentiate $Q$ with respect to $\theta$ using the chain rule.

$$Q = 5 - 5\sin(2\theta)$$

Using the derivative of sine, $\frac{d}{dx}(\sin(kx)) = k\cos(kx)$:
$$\frac{dQ}{d\theta} = -5(2\cos(2\theta))$$
$$\frac{dQ}{d\theta} = -10\cos(2\theta)$$

steps.title: Find critical values
steps.content:
To find the maximum and minimum values (extrema), we set the derivative equal to zero.

$$-10\cos(2\theta) = 0$$
$$\cos(2\theta) = 0$$

steps.title: Determine values for substitution
steps.content:
We need to substitute the critical conditions back into our expression for $Q$. Instead of solving for $\theta$, we can work directly with the trigonometric functions.

When $\cos(2\theta) = 0$, the angle $2\theta$ corresponds to the vertical axis on the unit circle (e.g., $\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3\pi}{2}$). At these points, the value of $\sin(2\theta)$ must be either $1$ or $-1$.

Using $\cos^2(2\theta) + \sin^2(2\theta) = 1$:
$$0^2 + \sin^2(2\theta) = 1 \implies \sin(2\theta) = \pm 1$$

steps.title: Calculate maximum and minimum values
steps.content:
Substitute the possible values of $\sin(2\theta)$ back into the simplified equation $Q = 5(1 - \sin(2\theta))$.

**Minimum Value:**
Occurs when $\sin(2\theta) = 1$:
$$Q_{\text{min}} = 5(1 - 1) = 0$$

**Maximum Value:**
Occurs when $\sin(2\theta) = -1$:
$$Q_{\text{max}} = 5(1 - (-1)) = 5(2) = 10$$

**Answer:**
Maximum = 10, Minimum = 0

parts.content: Hence find the range of \(\theta\) (in radians, \(0\le\theta<2\pi\)) for which the measured quantity \(Q\) is less than \(10\).
parts.markscheme:
- Set up inequality: \(5(1-\sin(2\theta))<10\Rightarrow 1-\sin(2\theta)<2\Rightarrow \sin(2\theta)>-1.\) <marks>M1</marks>
- The quantity is less than 10 everywhere except where \(Q=10\), i.e., \(\sin(2\theta)=-1\). <marks>M1</marks>
- Identify critical values: \(\sin(2\theta)=-1\Rightarrow 2\theta=\dfrac{3\pi}{2},\,\dfrac{7\pi}{2}.\)
- Divide by 2: \(\theta=\tfrac{3\pi}{4},\,\tfrac{7\pi}{4}.\)
- Determine valid range: \(\theta \in [0, 2\pi) \setminus \{\tfrac{3\pi}{4}, \tfrac{7\pi}{4}\}\). <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Set up the inequality
steps.content:
We are given the condition that the measured quantity $Q$ must be less than 10. Based on the previous parts of the question, the expression for $Q$ is $5(1 - \sin(2\theta))$.

Start by setting up the inequality:

$$5(1 - \sin(2\theta)) < 10$$

highlights.text: measured quantity Q is less than 10

steps.title: Isolate the trigonometric term
steps.content:
We solve the inequality for $\sin(2\theta)$ using algebraic manipulation:

$$\begin{aligned} 1 - \sin(2\theta) &< \frac{10}{5} \\ 1 - \sin(2\theta) &< 2 \\ -\sin(2\theta) &< 1 \end{aligned}$$

When we multiply or divide by a negative number ($-1$), we must flip the inequality sign:

$$\sin(2\theta) > -1$$

steps.title: Analyze the sine function range
steps.content:
Recall that the range of the sine function is always between $-1$ and $1$ inclusive ($-1 \le \sin(x) \le 1$).

Because $\sin(2\theta)$ is *always* greater than or equal to $-1$, the condition $\sin(2\theta) > -1$ is true for all angles **except** where $\sin(2\theta) = -1$.

Therefore, we simply need to find the values where $\sin(2\theta) = -1$ and exclude them from our domain.

steps.title: Find critical values for 2θ
steps.content:
We need to find $2\theta$ such that $\sin(2\theta) = -1$. First, determine the search domain for $2\theta$. 

Since the domain for $\theta$ is $0 \le \theta < 2\pi$, the domain for $2\theta$ is:

$$0 \le 2\theta < 4\pi$$

In the unit circle, $\sin(x) = -1$ at $x = \frac{3\pi}{2}$. We add $2\pi$ to find all solutions within the domain $0$ to $4\pi$:

$$\begin{aligned} 2\theta &= \frac{3\pi}{2} \\ 2\theta &= \frac{3\pi}{2} + 2\pi = \frac{7\pi}{2} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate sin(3π/2) and sin(7π/2) to confirm they equal -1

ti84.instructions: Calculate sin(3π/2) and sin(7π/2) to confirm they equal -1

desmos.instructions: Calculate sin(3π/2) and sin(7π/2) to confirm they equal -1

calculator.description: Verify the sine of the critical angles

steps.title: Solve for θ
steps.content:
Divide the values of $2\theta$ by 2 to find the critical values for $\theta$:

$$\begin{aligned} \theta &= \frac{3\pi}{4} \\ \theta &= \frac{7\pi}{4} \end{aligned}$$

These are the only two angles in the domain $[0, 2\pi)$ where $\sin(2\theta) = -1$.

steps.title: State the final range
steps.content:
Since the inequality holds for all values *except* these critical points, the valid range is the entire domain $0 \le \theta < 2\pi$ excluding these specific angles.

**Answer:**
$$0 \le \theta < 2\pi, \quad \theta \neq \frac{3\pi}{4}, \frac{7\pi}{4}$$

steps.title: Simplify the inequality
steps.content:
We are asked to find the range of $\theta$ where $Q < 10$. Using the expression for $Q$ (from the context of the question/markscheme), we set up the inequality:

$$5(1-\sin(2\theta)) < 10$$

Divide both sides by 5:

$$1-\sin(2\theta) < 2$$

Subtract 1 from both sides:

$$-\sin(2\theta) < 1$$

Multiply by $-1$ and flip the inequality sign:

$$\sin(2\theta) > -1$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate 5(1-sin(2*0)) to see if it is less than 10

calculator.description: Verify the inequality simplification logic numerically (e.g., test a value)

steps.title: Sketch the graph
steps.content:
To solve $\sin(2\theta) > -1$ using a graphical approach, we sketch the function $y = \sin(2\theta)$ and the boundary line $y = -1$ on the domain $0 \le \theta < 2\pi$.

The graph of $y = \sin(2\theta)$ is a sine wave with:
*   **Period**: $\dfrac{2\pi}{2} = \pi$ (so there are 2 full cycles in $2\pi$)
*   **Range**: $[-1, 1]$

Since the minimum value of the sine function is $-1$, the curve lies above or touches the line $y = -1$ everywhere. The inequality $\sin(2\theta) > -1$ means we must include all values **except** the points where the curve touches the line $y = -1$.

3. Enter y2 = -1
4. Set V-Window: x from 0 to 2π
5. Draw

3. Enter Y2 = -1

5. Press [GRAPH]

calculator.description: Graph the function and the boundary line

steps.title: Find intersection points
steps.content:
We need to find the specific values of $\theta$ where the minimum occurs, i.e., where $\sin(2\theta) = -1$.

The sine function equals $-1$ at $\dfrac{3\pi}{2}$ in the standard unit circle. Since our argument is $2\theta$, we solve:

$$2\theta = \frac{3\pi}{2} + 2k\pi$$

For the domain $0 \le \theta < 2\pi$, the argument $2\theta$ ranges from $0$ to $4\pi$. We look for values of $2\theta$ in this range:

1.  **First cycle**: $2\theta = \dfrac{3\pi}{2}$
2.  **Second cycle**: $2\theta = \dfrac{3\pi}{2} + 2\pi = \dfrac{7\pi}{2}$

gdc.instructions:
1. Select G-Solve (F5)
2. Select ISCT (Intersection)
3. Note the x-values

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC)
2. Select 5: intersect
3. Move cursor to the first minimum point and press [ENTER] three times
4. Repeat for the second minimum point

desmos.instructions: Click on the intersection points of the two graphs

steps.title: Calculate theta values
steps.content:
Now, divide the values for $2\theta$ by 2 to find $\theta$:

For the first point:
$$\theta = \frac{3\pi}{2} \div 2 = \frac{3\pi}{4}$$

For the second point:
$$\theta = \frac{7\pi}{2} \div 2 = \frac{7\pi}{4}$$

These are the only points where $Q = 10$. For all other $\theta$ in the domain, $Q < 10$.

steps.title: State the valid range
steps.content:
The quantity $Q$ is less than 10 for all values in the domain except at these two critical points. We exclude them from the interval $[0, 2\pi)$.

**Final Answer:**

$$0 \le \theta < 2\pi, \quad \theta \neq \frac{3\pi}{4}, \quad \theta \neq \frac{7\pi}{4}$$

Or in set notation:

$$\theta \in [0, 2\pi) \setminus \left\{\frac{3\pi}{4}, \frac{7\pi}{4}\right\}$$

parts.content: All possible values of $\cos\theta$ when $\sin\theta = -\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{5}}$.
parts.markscheme:
- From identity $\sin^2\theta+\cos^2\theta=1\Rightarrow \cos^2\theta=1-(-\tfrac{2}{\sqrt{5}})^2=1-\tfrac{4}{5}=\tfrac{1}{5}$. <marks>M1</marks>
- $\cos\theta=\pm\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$. <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: If $P$ lies in the third quadrant, find the exact values of $\sin(2\theta)$, $\cos(2\theta)$, and $\tan(2\theta)$.
parts.markscheme:
- In quadrant III: $\sin\theta=-\tfrac{2}{\sqrt{5}},\,\cos\theta=-\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$. <marks>M1</marks>
- $\sin(2\theta)=2\sin\theta\cos\theta=2(-\tfrac{2}{\sqrt{5}})(-\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}})=\tfrac{4}{5}.$ <marks>A1</marks>
- $\cos(2\theta)=\cos^2\theta-\sin^2\theta=\tfrac{1}{5}-\tfrac{4}{5}=-\tfrac{3}{5}.$ <marks>A1</marks>
- $\tan(2\theta)=\dfrac{\sin(2\theta)}{\cos(2\theta)}=\dfrac{4/5}{-3/5}=-\dfrac{4}{3}.$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Verify that the identity $\cot^2(2\theta)+1=\csc^2(2\theta)$ holds for your values.
parts.markscheme:
- LHS $=\cot^2(2\theta)+1=(-\tfrac{3}{4})^2+1=\tfrac{9}{16}+1=\tfrac{25}{16}.$ <marks>M1</marks>
- RHS $=\csc^2(2\theta)=\dfrac{1}{\sin^2(2\theta)}=\dfrac{1}{(4/5)^2}=\tfrac{25}{16}.$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content:
A rotating beacon's intensity $I$ is proportional to $(\sqrt{3}\sin\theta+\sqrt{3}\cos\theta)^2$.
Determine the **maximum** and **minimum** possible intensities, giving exact values.
parts.markscheme:
- Simplify: $I \propto 3(\sin\theta+\cos\theta)^2 = 3(1+\sin(2\theta)).$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Max when $\sin(2\theta)=1\Rightarrow I_\text{max}=3(1+1)=6.$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Min when $\sin(2\theta)=-1\Rightarrow I_\text{min}=3(1-1)=0.$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Hence find the range of $\theta$ (in radians, $0\le\theta<2\pi$) for which the intensity exceeds $4.5$.
parts.markscheme:
- $3(1+\sin(2\theta))>4.5\Rightarrow 1+\sin(2\theta)>1.5\Rightarrow \sin(2\theta)>0.5.$ <marks>M1</marks>
- $\sin(2\theta)=0.5\Rightarrow 2\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{6},\,\dfrac{5\pi}{6}.$
- Therefore $2\theta\in(\tfrac{\pi}{6},\tfrac{5\pi}{6})\cup(\tfrac{13\pi}{6},\tfrac{17\pi}{6})$. <marks>M1</marks>
- Divide by 2: $\theta\in\Bigl(\tfrac{\pi}{12},\tfrac{5\pi}{12}\Bigr)\cup\Bigl(\tfrac{13\pi}{12},\tfrac{17\pi}{12}\Bigr).$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content:
At a certain instant, the $y$-coordinate of $P$ is $\dfrac{5}{13}$.
Find all possible values of $\cos\theta$.
parts.markscheme:
- From identity $\sin^2\theta+\cos^2\theta=1 \Rightarrow \cos^2\theta=1-(\frac{5}{13})^2=\frac{144}{169}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- $\cos\theta=\pm\frac{12}{13}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the value of sine
steps.content:
The question defines the coordinates of point $P$ as $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$. Since the $y$-coordinate corresponds to $\sin\theta$, we can equate the given value to $\sin\theta$.

$$\sin\theta = \frac{5}{13}$$

steps.title: Select the Pythagorean identity
steps.content: To find $\cos\theta$ when $\sin\theta$ is known, we use the Pythagorean identity from the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet.

steps.title: Substitute and rearrange
steps.content:
Substitute $\sin\theta = \frac{5}{13}$ into the identity and rearrange the equation to solve for $\cos^2\theta$.

$$\begin{aligned} \cos^2\theta + \left(\frac{5}{13}\right)^2 &= 1 \\ \cos^2\theta + \frac{25}{169} &= 1 \\ \cos^2\theta &= 1 - \frac{25}{169} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the squared value
steps.content:
Perform the subtraction to find the value of $\cos^2\theta$.

$$\begin{aligned} \cos^2\theta &= \frac{169}{169} - \frac{25}{169} \\ \cos^2\theta &= \frac{144}{169} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression to verify the fraction:

steps.title: Solve for cosine
steps.content:
Take the square root of both sides to find $\cos\theta$. 

**Important:** When taking the square root of a variable squared (like $\cos^2\theta$), you must include both the positive and negative roots because the point $P$ could be in Quadrant I or Quadrant II.

$$\begin{aligned} \cos\theta &= \pm\sqrt{\frac{144}{169}} \\ \cos\theta &= \pm\frac{12}{13} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The possible values for $\cos\theta$ are:

$$\cos\theta = \frac{12}{13} \quad \text{or} \quad \cos\theta = -\frac{12}{13}$$

steps.title: Identify the sine value
steps.content:
The coordinates of point $P$ on the unit circle are defined as $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$. The question states that the $y$-coordinate is $\frac{5}{13}$.

Therefore, we know:
$$\sin\theta = \frac{5}{13}$$

steps.title: Construct a right-angled triangle
steps.content:
To find the cosine ratio, we can construct a right-angled triangle using the definition $\sin\theta = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Hypotenuse}}$.

We set the side lengths as:
*   $\text{Opposite} = 5$
*   $\text{Hypotenuse} = 13$

We need to find the **Adjacent** side, let's call it $a$.

steps.title: Calculate the adjacent side
steps.content:
Using Pythagoras' theorem ($a^2 + b^2 = c^2$), we can solve for the adjacent side $a$:

$$\begin{aligned} a^2 + 5^2 &= 13^2 \\ a^2 + 25 &= 169 \\ a^2 &= 169 - 25 \\ a^2 &= 144 \\ a &= \sqrt{144} \\ a &= 12 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate square root of (hypotenuse squared minus opposite squared)

ti84.instructions: Calculate square root of (hypotenuse squared minus opposite squared)

desmos.instructions: Calculate square root of (hypotenuse squared minus opposite squared)

calculator.description: Calculate the third side of the triangle using Pythagoras

steps.title: Determine the basic cosine ratio
steps.content:
Now we calculate the magnitude of the cosine ratio using $\cos\theta = \frac{\text{Adjacent}}{\text{Hypotenuse}}$.

With $\text{Adjacent} = 12$ and $\text{Hypotenuse} = 13$:
$$\cos\theta = \frac{12}{13}$$

steps.title: Determine possible quadrants
steps.content:
We must check which quadrants are possible. We are given that $\sin\theta = \frac{5}{13}$, which is a **positive** value.

On the unit circle:
*   Sine ($y$) is positive in **Quadrant 1** and **Quadrant 2**.
*   In Quadrant 1, cosine ($x$) is **positive**.
*   In Quadrant 2, cosine ($x$) is **negative**.

Therefore, $\cos\theta$ can be either positive or negative.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Combining the ratio with the possible signs, the possible values for $\cos\theta$ are:

$$\cos\theta = \pm\frac{12}{13}$$

parts.content: If $P$ lies in the **second** quadrant, find the exact values of $\sin(2\theta)$, $\cos(2\theta)$, and $\tan(2\theta)$.
parts.markscheme:
- In quadrant II: $\sin\theta=\frac{5}{13},\,\cos\theta=-\frac{12}{13}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- $\sin(2\theta)=2\sin\theta\cos\theta=2(\frac{5}{13})(-\frac{12}{13})=-\frac{120}{169}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- $\cos(2\theta)=1-2\sin^2\theta=1-2(\frac{25}{169})=\frac{119}{169}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- $\tan(2\theta)=\dfrac{\sin(2\theta)}{\cos(2\theta)}=\dfrac{-120/169}{119/169}=-\frac{120}{119}$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
From the problem context (implied by the markscheme and typical question structure), we are working with the value:

$$\sin\theta = \frac{5}{13}$$

We need to find the exact values of $\sin(2\theta)$, $\cos(2\theta)$, and $\tan(2\theta)$ for a point $P$ in the **second** quadrant.

steps.title: Find cos(θ)
steps.content:
First, we find $\cos\theta$ using the Pythagorean identity from the data booklet. We substitute $\sin\theta = \frac{5}{13}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta &= 1 \\ \cos^2\theta + \left(\frac{5}{13}\right)^2 &= 1 \\ \cos^2\theta + \frac{25}{169} &= 1 \\ \cos^2\theta &= 1 - \frac{25}{169} \\ \cos^2\theta &= \frac{144}{169} \end{aligned}$$

Since $P$ is in the **second quadrant**, the $x$-coordinate ($\cos\theta$) must be negative:

$$\cos\theta = -\sqrt{\frac{144}{169}} = -\frac{12}{13}$$

steps.title: Calculate sin(2θ)
steps.content:
Now we use the double angle identity for sine found in the data booklet. We substitute $\sin\theta = \frac{5}{13}$ and $\cos\theta = -\frac{12}{13}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \sin(2\theta) &= 2\sin\theta\cos\theta \\ &= 2\left(\frac{5}{13}\right)\left(-\frac{12}{13}\right) \\ &= \frac{-120}{169} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate cos(2θ)
steps.content:
Next, we use the double angle identity for cosine. Using the formula from the data booklet:

$$\begin{aligned} \cos(2\theta) &= \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta \\ &= \left(-\frac{12}{13}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{5}{13}\right)^2 \\ &= \frac{144}{169} - \frac{25}{169} \\ &= \frac{119}{169} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate tan(2θ)
steps.content:
Finally, we find $\tan(2\theta)$ by dividing our results for sine and cosine:

$$\begin{aligned} \tan(2\theta) &= \frac{\sin(2\theta)}{\cos(2\theta)} \\ &= \frac{-\frac{120}{169}}{\frac{119}{169}} \\ &= -\frac{120}{119} \end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\sin(2\theta) = -\frac{120}{169}, \quad \cos(2\theta) = \frac{119}{169}, \quad \tan(2\theta) = -\frac{120}{119}$$

parts.content: Verify that the identity $\cot^2(2\theta)+1=\csc^2(2\theta)$ holds for your values.

steps.title: Identify values from previous parts
steps.content:
To verify the identity, we first retrieve the fractional values for $\cot(2\theta)$ and $\sin(2\theta)$ calculated in the previous parts of the question:

$$\cot(2\theta) = -\frac{119}{120}$$

$$\sin(2\theta) = -\frac{120}{169}$$

steps.title: Substitute into LHS
steps.content:
We substitute the value of $\cot(2\theta)$ into the left-hand side (LHS) of the identity $\cot^2(2\theta)+1$:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{LHS} &= \left(-\frac{119}{120}\right)^2 + 1 \\ &= \frac{14161}{14400} + 1 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Evaluate LHS
steps.content:
Now we combine the terms by finding a common denominator:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{LHS} &= \frac{14161}{14400} + \frac{14400}{14400} \\ &= \frac{28561}{14400} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute into RHS
steps.content:
Next, we substitute the value of $\sin(2\theta)$ into the right-hand side (RHS). Note that $\csc(2\theta) = \frac{1}{\sin(2\theta)}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{RHS} &= \csc^2(2\theta) \\ &= \left(\frac{1}{\sin(2\theta)}\right)^2 \\ &= \left(\frac{1}{-120/169}\right)^2 \\ &= \left(-\frac{169}{120}\right)^2 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Evaluate RHS
steps.content:
We square the value to find the final fraction for the RHS:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{RHS} &= \frac{169^2}{120^2} \\ &= \frac{28561}{14400} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content:
Comparing the results:

$$\text{LHS} = \frac{28561}{14400} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{RHS} = \frac{28561}{14400}$$ 

Since $\text{LHS} = \text{RHS}$, the identity is verified.

steps.title: Rewrite the identity
steps.content:
First, we rewrite the identity $\cot^2(2\theta)+1=\csc^2(2\theta)$ using the definitions in terms of sine and cosine:

$$\left(\frac{\cos(2\theta)}{\sin(2\theta)}\right)^2 + 1 = \frac{1}{\sin^2(2\theta)}$$

steps.title: Identify values from previous parts
steps.content:
From your previous working, recall the values for $\sin(2\theta)$ and $\cos(2\theta)$:

$$\sin(2\theta) = -\frac{120}{169} \quad \text{and} \quad \cos(2\theta) = \frac{119}{169}$$

steps.title: Substitute into LHS
steps.content:
Substitute these values into the Left Hand Side (LHS) expression:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{LHS} &= \left(\frac{119/169}{-120/169}\right)^2 + 1 \\ &= \left(-\frac{119}{120}\right)^2 + 1 \\ &= \frac{14161}{14400} + \frac{14400}{14400} \\ &= \frac{28561}{14400} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute into RHS
steps.content:
Substitute the value of $\sin(2\theta)$ into the Right Hand Side (RHS) expression:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{RHS} &= \frac{1}{(-120/169)^2} \\ &= \frac{1}{14400/28561} \\ &= \frac{28561}{14400} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content:
Since both sides equate to $\frac{28561}{14400}$, the identity is verified.

$$\text{LHS} = \text{RHS}$$

parts.content:
A rotating light beam at $P$ has intensity $I$ given by $I = (\sin\theta-\cos\theta)^2$.
As $\theta$ increases from $0$ to $2\pi$, determine the **maximum** and **minimum** possible intensities, giving exact values.
parts.markscheme:
- Expand: $(\sin\theta-\cos\theta)^2=1-2\sin\theta\cos\theta=1-\sin(2\theta)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Max when $\sin(2\theta)=-1 \Rightarrow I_{\text{max}}=1-(-1)=2$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Min when $\sin(2\theta)=1 \Rightarrow I_{\text{min}}=1-1=0$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Expand the intensity function
steps.content:
First, we expand the squared expression for the intensity $I$ using the algebraic identity $(a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$:

$$I = (\sin\theta - \cos\theta)^2 = \sin^2\theta - 2\sin\theta\cos\theta + \cos^2\theta$$

steps.title: Apply Pythagorean identity
steps.content:
We can group the $\sin^2\theta$ and $\cos^2\theta$ terms together. From the data booklet, we know that $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$. 

Substituting this into our equation:

$$\begin{aligned} I &= (\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta) - 2\sin\theta\cos\theta \\ I &= 1 - 2\sin\theta\cos\theta \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Apply double angle identity
steps.content:
Next, we recognize the term $2\sin\theta\cos\theta$ as the double angle identity for sine. Using the formula from the data booklet:

$$I = 1 - \sin(2\theta)$$

steps.title: Analyze the range
steps.content:
We know that the sine function, $\sin(x)$, oscillates between $-1$ and $1$. 

Since $\theta$ increases from $0$ to $2\pi$, the angle $2\theta$ ranges from $0$ to $4\pi$, which covers two full cycles. This ensures $\sin(2\theta)$ reaches both its maximum and minimum values:

$$-1 \leq \sin(2\theta) \leq 1$$

2. Enter f1(x) = 1 - sin(2x)
3. Set window settings for x from 0 to 2pi

3. Press [WINDOW] and set Xmin=0, Xmax=6.28 (2pi), Ymin=-1, Ymax=3
4. Press [GRAPH]

2. Observe the wave oscillates between y=0 and y=2

calculator.description: Visualize the function I = 1 - sin(2x) to see the range

steps.title: Calculate maximum intensity
steps.content:
To maximize $I = 1 - \sin(2\theta)$, we need to subtract the smallest possible value. The minimum value of $\sin(2\theta)$ is $-1$.

$$I_{\text{max}} = 1 - (-1) = 2$$

steps.title: Calculate minimum intensity
steps.content:
To minimize $I = 1 - \sin(2\theta)$, we need to subtract the largest possible value. The maximum value of $\sin(2\theta)$ is $1$.

$$I_{\text{min}} = 1 - (1) = 0$$

steps.title: Identify the expression
steps.content:
We need to find the maximum and minimum values of the intensity function:

$$I = (\sin\theta - \cos\theta)^2$$

To make this easier to analyze without expanding, we will express the term inside the brackets, $\sin\theta - \cos\theta$, in the harmonic form $$R\sin(\theta - \alpha)$$.

steps.title: Set up the Harmonic Form
steps.content:
We want to match our expression to the compound angle formula for sine:

$$R\sin(\theta - \alpha) = R(\sin\theta\cos\alpha - \cos\theta\sin\alpha)$$

Comparing this to $$1\sin\theta - 1\cos\theta$$, we equate the coefficients:

$$\begin{aligned} R\cos\alpha &= 1 \\ R\sin\alpha &= 1 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate R and α
steps.content:
First, we find $$R$$ using the Pythagorean identity:

$$R = \sqrt{(1)^2 + (1)^2} = \sqrt{2}$$

Next, we find the auxiliary angle $$\alpha$$:

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{R\sin\alpha}{R\cos\alpha} = \frac{1}{1} = 1$$

Since sine and cosine coefficients are positive in our setup, $$\alpha$$ is in the first quadrant:

$$\alpha = \frac{\pi}{4}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the square root and inverse tangent.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the square root and inverse tangent.

calculator.description: Calculate R and the angle alpha

steps.title: Rewrite the Intensity function
steps.content:
Substituting $$R=\sqrt{2}$$ and $$\alpha=\frac{\pi}{4}$$ back into our harmonic form:

$$\sin\theta - \cos\theta = \sqrt{2}\sin\left(\theta - \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$

Now substitute this back into the equation for intensity $$I$$:

$$\begin{aligned} I &= \left[\sqrt{2}\sin\left(\theta - \frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right]^2 \\ I &= 2\sin^2\left(\theta - \frac{\pi}{4}\right) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine the Maximum Intensity
steps.content:
To find the **maximum** value, we analyze the range of the sine squared function. The maximum value of $$\sin^2(x)$$ is $$1$$.

$$I_{\text{max}} = 2(1) = 2$$

This occurs when $$\sin(\theta - \frac{\pi}{4}) = \pm 1$$, which is possible within the given domain $$0 \le \theta \le 2\pi$$.

steps.title: Determine the Minimum Intensity
steps.content:
To find the **minimum** value, we note that the square of any real number is non-negative. The minimum value of $$\sin^2(x)$$ is $$0$$.

$$I_{\text{min}} = 2(0) = 0$$

This occurs when $$\sin(\theta - \frac{\pi}{4}) = 0$$, which is also possible within the domain.

steps.title: Verify with Calculator
steps.content: We can verify these extrema by graphing the original function $$I = (\sin x - \cos x)^2$$ on the interval $$0 \le x \le 2\pi$$. The graph will oscillate between $$y=0$$ and $$y=2$$.

gdc.instructions: Graph y=(sin(x)-cos(x))^2. Set View Window x from 0 to 2π. Use G-Solve to find Max and Min.

desmos.instructions: Graph the function and click on the peaks and troughs to see the y-values.

calculator.description: Graph the function to identify maximum and minimum values

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The maximum and minimum intensities are:

Maximum: $$2$$
Minimum: $$0$$

steps.title: Identify the function
steps.content:
The problem asks us to find the maximum and minimum intensities for the function $I$ as $\theta$ ranges from $0$ to $2\pi$. We start with the given equation:

$$I = (\sin\theta-\cos\theta)^2$$

steps.title: Simplify using identities
steps.content:
To make differentiation easier, we expand the squared term and use trigonometric identities from the data booklet. 

$$I = \sin^2\theta - 2\sin\theta\cos\theta + \cos^2\theta$$

Substituting $$\cos^2\theta+\sin^2\theta=1$$ and $$\sin 2\theta=2\sin\theta\cos\theta$$:

$$I(\theta) = 1 - \sin(2\theta)$$

steps.title: Differentiate with respect to theta
steps.content:
Next, we find the derivative of $I$ with respect to $\theta$ (denoted as $\frac{dI}{d\theta}$) to locate the stationary points. Using the chain rule on the $\sin(2\theta)$ term:

$$\frac{dI}{d\theta} = \frac{d}{d\theta}(1) - \frac{d}{d\theta}(\sin(2\theta))$$

$$\frac{dI}{d\theta} = 0 - 2\cos(2\theta)$$

$$\frac{dI}{d\theta} = -2\cos(2\theta)$$

steps.title: Solve for critical values
steps.content:
To find the extrema (maximum and minimum), we set the derivative equal to zero:

$$-2\cos(2\theta) = 0$$
$$\cos(2\theta) = 0$$

For $0 \le \theta \le 2\pi$, the angle $2\theta$ ranges from $0$ to $4\pi$. The cosine function is zero at odd multiples of $\frac{\pi}{2}$ ($90^\circ$):

$$2\theta = \frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3\pi}{2}, \frac{5\pi}{2}, \frac{7\pi}{2}$$

Dividing by 2 gives the critical values for $\theta$:

$$\theta = \frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{3\pi}{4}, \frac{5\pi}{4}, \frac{7\pi}{4}$$

5. Use G-SOLV > MAX and MIN to find coordinates
